close_reason: Architecture pivot complete: Gradio + LlamaIndex RAG fully integrated, all child tasks completed, performance targets met.
description: DECISION: Pivot from Dash synchronous callbacks to Gradio async interface with proper RAG pipeline. Dash approach blocked by: 70s response times, UI callback DOM conflicts, no streaming support. Gradio provides: async/streaming, built-in chat UI, better UX. LlamaIndex provides: vector store, semantic search, embeddings. Target: <3s simple responses, <10s complex.
notes: **BLOCKED**: Integration not working - AI assistant shows 'Starting AI assistant...' and continues checking status forever (attempt 14+). Root cause identified: data/ai_assistant/__init__.py imports deleted modules causing ModuleNotFoundError. Fix in progress: burndown-chart-017.115.5.8
title: Architecture pivot: Replace Dash UI with Gradio + LlamaIndex RAG

close_reason: Completed Gradio integration with custom theme. Server auto-starts, new tab approach, Bootstrap blue with Inter font. CSS work complete.
description:
IMPLEMENTATION PLAN:

1. Remove obsolete code:
   - Delete callbacks/ai_assistant.py (563 lines - uses Phi-3, ModelManager, ResponseGenerator)
   - Delete data/ai_assistant/model_manager.py, response_generator.py, context_builder.py, prompt_templates.py
   - Keep data/ai_assistant/chat_persistence.py (for future use)
   - Delete ui/ai_assistant.py (old Dash UI components)
   - Delete assets/ai_assistant.css, assets/ai_assistant.js

2. Create new Gradio integration:
   - NEW: callbacks/ai_chatbot.py - Simple callback to open Gradio in iframe/window
   - NEW: ui/ai_chatbot_panel.py - Collapsible panel in top-right (toast position)
   - Style matching: Use app colors, same position as app-notifications (top: 10px, right: 10px)
   - Collapsible: Header with robot icon + minimize/maximize buttons
   - Non-blocking: Gradio iframe loads in background, renders when ready

3. Architecture:
   - Button click → Open collapsible panel at top-right
   - Panel contains iframe embedding http://127.0.0.1:7860
   - Panel z-index: 9998 (below toasts at 9999)
   - Minimize: Collapse to small tab showing 'AI Assistant' + unread indicator
   - Maximize: Show full 400x600px iframe with chat
   - Background operation: Gradio server runs independently, survives panel close

4. UI Flow:
   - Click 'AI Assistant' button → Panel slides in from top-right
   - Panel header: 'AI Assistant' + minimize/close buttons
   - Panel body: Gradio iframe (seamless, no borders)
   - Close button: Hide panel, keep Gradio server running
   - Minimize: Shrink to 40px height tab

Technical decisions:
- Use iframe (not separate window) for seamless UX
- Gradio server auto-starts on first button click (lazy loading)
- Panel component: dbc.Offcanvas with custom positioning
- Communication: None needed (Gradio is self-contained)
- Styling: Match dashboard theme with custom CSS
title: Integrate Gradio interface with Dash app

close_reason: Completed: Created GradioServerManager with lifecycle management and tests
description:
Create service to manage Gradio server lifecycle:

NEW FILE: data/gradio_server_manager.py

RESPONSIBILITIES:
1. Server lifecycle:
   - start_server() - Launch Gradio on port 7860 if not running
   - stop_server() - Gracefully shutdown Gradio
   - is_running() - Check if server is available (health check)
   - get_server_url() - Return http://127.0.0.1:7860

2. Implementation:
   - Use subprocess.Popen to launch gradio in background
   - Command: python -m data.ai_assistant.langchain_rag_chat
   - Store PID for graceful shutdown
   - Health check: HTTP GET to /health endpoint (or test main URL)
   - Retry logic: 3 attempts with 1s delay

3. Integration:
   - Called by callbacks/ai_chatbot.py on first button click
   - Singleton pattern (only one server instance)
   - Auto-start on app launch (optional, via app.py initialization)
   - Cleanup on app shutdown (register atexit handler)

TECHNICAL:
- Use requests library for health checks
- Timeout: 5s for health check
- Log all server lifecycle events
- Handle port conflicts (detect if 7860 in use)
title: Create Gradio chatbot management service

close_reason: Fixed interval disabled state management. Panel now properly enables status polling when opening and self-disables when server ready or panel closes. All 16 tests passing.
description:
Create callbacks to manage chatbot panel state:

NEW FILE: callbacks/ai_chatbot.py

CALLBACKS:

1. toggle_chatbot_panel() - Open/close panel
   Input: 'ai-assistant-button' n_clicks
   Input: 'ai-chatbot-close' n_clicks
   State: 'ai-chatbot-state' data (is_open, is_minimized)
   Output: 'ai-chatbot-panel' style (display, height)
   Output: 'ai-chatbot-state' data (updated state)
   
   Logic:
   - First click: Start Gradio server (via GradioServerManager)
   - Show panel with loading state
   - Health check Gradio (retry 3x with 1s delay)
   - When ready: Update iframe src
   - Close: Hide panel (keep server running)

2. minimize_chatbot_panel() - Minimize/maximize
   Input: 'ai-chatbot-minimize' n_clicks
   State: 'ai-chatbot-state' data
   Output: 'ai-chatbot-panel' style (height)
   Output: 'ai-chatbot-minimize' children (icon switch)
   Output: 'ai-chatbot-state' data
   
   Logic:
   - Toggle between 50px (minimized) and 650px (expanded)
   - Switch icon: fa-minus ↔ fa-expand-alt

3. chatbot_iframe_status() - Update iframe based on server status
   Input: Interval component (every 5s, only when panel open)
   State: 'ai-chatbot-state' data
   Output: 'ai-chatbot-iframe-container' children
   
   Logic:
   - Check if Gradio server is running
   - If running: Show iframe
   - If not running: Show error message + retry button

STORES:
- ai-chatbot-state: {is_open: bool, is_minimized: bool, server_ready: bool}

ERROR HANDLING:
- Log all server communication failures
- Show user-friendly error messages
- Provide manual retry button
title: Create AI chatbot callbacks for panel control

close_reason: Fixed __init__.py imports and removed all emoji (Rule #6 violation) that caused UnicodeEncodeError on Windows
description:
The data/ai_assistant/__init__.py file still imports deleted modules (model_manager, prompt_templates) that were removed during the Gradio refactoring. This causes ModuleNotFoundError when the Gradio server tries to start.

**Root Cause:**
- Bead 017.115.5.1 deleted obsolete Phi-3 code (model_manager.py, prompt_templates.py, etc.)
- But data/ai_assistant/__init__.py was not updated
- When Gradio server starts via 'python -m data.ai_assistant.langchain_rag_chat', it fails

**Error:**
\\\
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'data.ai_assistant.model_manager'
\\\

**Fix:**
Remove imports for deleted modules from __init__.py:
- Remove: from data.ai_assistant.model_manager import ModelManager
- Remove: from data.ai_assistant.prompt_templates import PromptTemplates
- Keep: ChatMessage, ChatPersistence, KnowledgeLoader (still exist)

**Verification:**
Run: python -m data.ai_assistant.langchain_rag_chat
Should start without import errors.
title: Fix data.ai_assistant __init__.py imports after module cleanup

description:
CRITICAL: Break down jira_simple.py from 3,593 lines into semantic modules following architectural guidelines (500 line max).

FILE ANALYSIS:
Current: 3,593 lines (718% over 500 line limit)
Target: ~8-10 modules of <500 lines each

SEMANTIC GROUPS IDENTIFIED:
1. Configuration & Constants (~50 lines)
2. Field & Config Management (~250 lines)
3. Issue Count & Cache Validation (~200 lines)
4. Two-Phase Fetch Optimization (~400 lines)
5. Core Pagination Logic (~350 lines)
6. Main Fetch Function (~500 lines)
7. Changelog Fetching (~400 lines)
8. Delta Fetch & Merging (~400 lines)
9. Cache Persistence (~350 lines)
10. Data Transformation (~200 lines)
11. High-Level Sync Functions (~500 lines)

PROPOSED MODULE STRUCTURE:
data/jira/
├── __init__.py              # Public API exports
├── config.py                # Config loading, validation, hash generation
├── cache_validator.py       # Cache validation, size checks, status
├── field_utils.py           # Field extraction, mappings

REFACTORING STRATEGY:

description:
Add UI configuration for team working schedules AND update all time-based calculations to use working days instead of calendar days.

## Problem
- Current system assumes all teams work Mon-Fri AND hardcodes 7-day denominators
- Teams working weekends (24/7 ops, support, Middle East regions) have inaccurate metrics
- All calculations divide by 7 (calendar days) instead of actual working days
- 'Items per week' means 'items per calendar week' - should be 'items per working week'
- No way to configure custom working patterns

## Solution (Phase 2.2)

### Part 1: Configuration UI
Add 'Working Schedule' tab in Settings → Field Mappings → Types modal with:
- Presets: Standard (Mon-Fri), 7-Day Operations, Custom
- Per-day checkboxes for working status
- Optional per-day weight sliders (0.0-2.0) for uneven completion patterns
- Default: Mon-Fri with weight 1.0 each

### Part 2: System-Wide Calculation Changes (CRITICAL)
Replace all hardcoded time denominators with working_schedule-derived values:

**Example Impact (5-day vs 7-day team):**
- Current: 20 items / 7 days = 2.86 items/day
- Should be (5-day): 20 items / 5 working days = 4.0 items/day
- Should be (7-day): 20 items / 7 working days = 2.86 items/day

**Files with Hardcoded '/ 7' to Replace:**
- data/processing.py:367 - \days_per_week = 7.0\ → \days_per_week = get_working_days_per_week()\
- data/budget_calculator.py:841 - \elapsed_weeks = elapsed_days / 7.0\
- data/dora_metrics.py:649 - \weeks = time_period_days / 7.0\
- visualization/charts.py:423, 433 - PERT time weeks conversion
- visualization/helpers.py:218, 228 - Completion time calculations
- ui/budget_cards/timeline_cards.py:102-103, 658 - Timeline calculations
- ui/budget_timeline_compact.py:98-99, 103, 366 - Weeks elapsed
- ui/dashboard_enhanced.py:856, 1099 - Weeks to deadline
- configuration/dora_config.py:218 - Benchmark conversions

**Calculation Standardization Required:**
1. Create \configuration/working_schedule.py\:
   - \get_working_days_per_week(schedule: Dict) -> float\
   - \get_working_days_count(schedule: Dict, start: date, end: date) -> float\
   - \get_week_progress(schedule: Dict) -> float\

2. Update all functions to accept \working_schedule\ parameter
3. Replace hardcoded 7 with dynamic calculation
4. Update tests to validate 5-day vs 7-day scenarios

## Configuration Schema
\\\json
{
  'working_schedule': {
    'monday': {'working': true, 'weight': 1.0},
    'tuesday': {'working': true, 'weight': 1.0},
    'wednesday': {'working': true, 'weight': 1.0},
    'thursday': {'working': true, 'weight': 1.0},
    'friday': {'working': true, 'weight': 1.0},
    'saturday': {'working': false, 'weight': 0.0},
    'sunday': {'working': false, 'weight': 0.0}
  }
}
\\\

## Team Examples
- Traditional (US/Europe): Mon-Fri → 5 working days/week
- 7-Day Ops (Support): Mon-Sun → 7 working days/week
- Middle East: Sun-Thu → 5 working days/week
- Flexible: Tue-Sat → 5 working days/week
- Weighted: Mon-Fri with Thu/Fri heavier (1.2, 1.5) → ~5.4 working weight/week
- Crunch: Temporary override for sprint → configurable

## Affected Calculations (System-Wide)
**ALL calculations that divide by time must use working schedule:**
- Flow Velocity (items per working week)
- Flow Time (working days, not calendar days)
- Flow Efficiency (working time / total working time)
- DORA Deployment Frequency (per working week)
- DORA Lead Time (working days)
- All rolling averages (4 working weeks)
- PERT forecasts (working days to completion)
- Budget burn rate (per working week)
- Velocity projections (working days)
- Daily rates (per working day)

## Migration & Recalculation
- **User changes config → clicks Update Data → ALL metrics recalculate**
- Same as other config changes (query, field mappings, etc.)
- Historical data uses new denominator (retroactive normalization)
- Charts/graphs automatically reflect working days

## Implementation Files
- **New**: configuration/working_schedule.py (schema, validation, calculations)
- **New**: ui/settings/working_schedule.py (UI component with presets)
- **Enhanced**: data/persistence/ (add to project settings)
- **Enhanced**: callbacks/settings_panel.py (add working schedule tab)
- **Enhanced**: data/processing.py (replace days_per_week = 7.0)
- **Enhanced**: data/budget_calculator.py (use working days)
- **Enhanced**: data/dora_metrics.py (use working weeks)
- **Enhanced**: visualization/charts.py (PERT with working days)
- **Enhanced**: visualization/helpers.py (working days calculations)
- **Enhanced**: ui/budget_cards/*.py (timeline with working weeks)
- **Enhanced**: ui/dashboard_enhanced.py (deadline with working weeks)
- **Enhanced**: configuration/dora_config.py (benchmarks per working week)
- **New**: tests/unit/data/test_working_schedule.py (5-day vs 7-day)
- **New**: tests/integration/test_working_schedule_impact.py (system-wide validation)
- **Doc**: docs/configuration_guide.md (working schedule impact)
- **Doc**: docs/flow_metrics.md (calculation with working days)

## Edge Cases
- Holidays: treat as non-working (0 weight)
- Partial days: use fractional weights (0.5)
- Timezone: use server local time for day-of-week
- Zero working days: validation prevents (minimum 1 day)

## UI Location
Settings → Field Mappings → (New Tab) 'Working Schedule'

## Testing Strategy
- Unit tests: 5-day, 6-day, 7-day schedules
- Integration: Verify velocity changes (20 items / 5 days vs / 7 days)
- Regression: All metrics use working days consistently
- Visual: Charts show 'working weeks' in labels
- Edge cases: Weighted days, cultural variations

## Success Criteria
- ✅ Zero hardcoded '/ 7' denominators remain
- ✅ Metrics change when working_schedule changes
- ✅ 5-day team shows higher daily velocity than 7-day team (same items)
- ✅ Update Data recalculates all metrics
- ✅ Charts labeled with 'working days/weeks'

Timeline: 2-3 days (architectural change + UI)
Complexity: High (system-wide refactor)
Priority: 1 (High - affects all teams, especially weekend workers)
title: Configurable Working Schedule for Teams

description:
Users have difficulty identifying which query is currently active and when its data was last updated.

## Current State
- Active indicator: '(Active)' text postfix attached to query name in dropdown
- No visibility into when data was last refreshed
- Users must manually track when they last updated data

## Problem
Users need to quickly identify:
1. Which query is currently active
2. When was the data last updated (important for stale data awareness)

## Solution: Compact Relative Timestamp Display
Add last update timestamp after [Active] postfix using compact relative format:

**Format**: Query Name [Active] • 2h ago

**Examples**:
- Last 12 Weeks [Active] • 2h ago
- Development Sprint • 5d ago
- Bugs Query [Active] • Just now
- Old Query • Jan 28

**Relative time ranges**:
- Just now (< 1 minute)
- 5m ago (1-59 minutes)
- 2h ago (1-23 hours)
- 3d ago (1-6 days)
- 2w ago (7-28 days)
- Jan 15 (>28 days, same year)
- Dec '25 (previous years)

**Timestamp Source**:
- Use jira_cache.created_at from database
- Represents START of update operation (when data snapshot begins)
- Only shown for queries with fetched data (never-fetched queries show no timestamp)

## Implementation Scope
1. Add get_relative_time_string() helper in data/query_manager.py
2. Enhance list_queries_with_last_update() to join with jira_cache.created_at
3. Update dropdown rendering in 13 files (ui/query_selector.py, callbacks/*, etc.)
4. Add mobile CSS for graceful text wrapping on small screens
5. Add unit tests for relative time formatting

## Mobile Considerations
- **Character budget**: ~10-12 chars added per query name
- **320px screens**: Text wraps gracefully to 2 lines (CSS word-wrap)
- **Responsive font**: 13px on <375px screens for better fit
- **Visual separator**: Bullet (•) creates clear hierarchy
- **No icon fallback needed**: Testing will confirm

## Goals
- Instant awareness of data freshness
- Compact display that works on mobile (320px+)
- Intuitive relative time format
- No timestamp for never-fetched queries
notes: Implemented Option 1: Compact relative time display in query dropdowns. Timestamps show after [Active] postfix using format '• 2h ago' or '• Jan 28'. Created time_formatting.py utility, updated 13+ files, added mobile CSS, and created 18 unit tests (all passing). Timestamp represents operation START time from jira_cache.created_at.
title: Enhance active query visual indicator in Queries tab

description:
Refactor data/metrics_calculator.py from 1666 lines to comply with 500-line architectural guideline.

## Current Analysis
**File**: data/metrics_calculator.py
**Size**: 1666 lines (3.3x over 500-line limit)
**Functions**: 6 main calculation functions

## Initial Assessment
Contains metric calculation functions:
1. **get_current_iso_week()** - Utility (< 20 lines)
2. **calculate_and_save_weekly_metrics()** - Main weekly calculation (likely 1400+ lines)
3. **calculate_metrics_for_last_n_weeks()** - Historical calculation (< 200 lines)
4. **calculate_forecast()** - Forecast calculation (< 150 lines)
5. **calculate_trend_vs_forecast()** - Trend analysis (< 150 lines)
6. **calculate_flow_load_range()** - Flow metrics (< 150 lines)

The calculate_and_save_weekly_metrics function is likely monolithic.

## Proposed Split Strategy
1. **data/metrics/** subdirectory:
   - weekly_calculator.py - Main weekly metrics calculation (< 450 lines)
   - historical_calculator.py - calculate_metrics_for_last_n_weeks (< 250 lines)
   - forecast_calculator.py - calculate_forecast, calculate_trend_vs_forecast (< 350 lines)
   - flow_calculator.py - calculate_flow_load_range + flow-specific metrics (< 300 lines)
   - helpers.py - get_current_iso_week + utility functions (< 200 lines)
   
2. If calculate_and_save_weekly_metrics is too large, further split:
   - weekly_calculator.py - Main orchestration (< 300 lines)
   - scope_calculator.py - Scope-related calculations (< 350 lines)
   - velocity_calculator.py - Velocity and trend calculations (< 350 lines)
   - quality_calculator.py - Bug and quality metrics (< 300 lines)

## Tasks
1. Read calculate_and_save_weekly_metrics to identify sub-calculations
2. Extract independent metric calculations
3. Create calculation pipeline pattern
4. Split into 5-8 focused files (each < 500 lines)
5. Update imports in data/__init__.py and callbacks
6. Run tests to verify metrics accuracy

## Success Criteria
- All files < 500 lines (target: 250-400)
- Clear metric domain separation
- No calculation changes
- All tests pass
- Zero errors from get_errors

description: On first run of the packaged executable, extract the project's LICENSE.txt file to the application directory alongside the exe. This ensures users have easy access to license terms without needing to open the executable bundle. Implementation: Check if LICENSE.txt exists in current directory on startup. If not found and running as frozen executable, extract from bundled resources. Place LICENSE.txt in same directory as BurndownChart.exe. Log extraction for troubleshooting. Note: Third-party licenses are bundled inside the executable and accessible via PyInstaller resource system. Legal requirement is typically to make them ACCESSIBLE not necessarily extract them. They remain in bundle and can be accessed programmatically if needed. Related: Portable executable - first run acts as installation/setup.

close_reason: Fixed race condition by forcing page reload after update completes
description: After update from 2.7.16 to 2.7.17, the overlay disappears correctly but the page does NOT auto-refresh. Footer version and success toast only appear after manual browser refresh. Expected: page should auto-refresh or Dash should auto-reconnect and trigger UI updates.
notes:
INVESTIGATION FINDINGS:

**Current Behavior:**
1. User clicks Update button
2. Overlay shows 'Reconnecting...'
3. Update completes, overlay disappears
4. Page stays on old version (footer still shows v2.7.16)
5. No success toast shown
6. Manual refresh → footer updates to v2.7.17 + success toast appears

**Expected Behavior:**
1. User clicks Update button
2. Overlay shows
3. Update completes
4. Overlay disappears
5. Page auto-refreshes OR Dash auto-reconnects
6. Footer shows v2.7.17
7. Success toast displays immediately

**TIMING-DEPENDENT BEHAVIOR (Critical Finding):**

Test 1 - Fast Machine (original report):
- Toast: NOT shown
- Footer: NOT updated
- After manual refresh: Both toast and footer appear

Test 2 - Slow Machine:
- Toast: SHOWN correctly
- Footer: NOT updated
- After manual refresh: Footer updates, toast does NOT show again (correct)

**Analysis:**
This confirms a RACE CONDITION between JavaScript DOM manipulation and Dash reconnection:
- JavaScript tries to update footer + show toast (lines 293-310)
- Dash websocket reconnects and re-renders components
- On fast machines: Dash reconnects before JavaScript executes → both fail
- On slow machines: JavaScript toast succeeds, but Dash still overwrites footer before it can update

**Root Cause:**
The code removes the overlay (line 293) which allows Dash to begin reconnection. Dash then re-renders the entire page/components, OVERWRITING any JavaScript DOM changes made between overlay removal and full reconnection.

The JavaScript manipulations (footer update, toast creation) are fighting against Dash's component re-rendering. They only work if executed after Dash finishes reconnecting, which is unpredictable timing.

**Code Location:**
- assets/update_reconnect.js lines 270-330 (pollServer function, update flow branch)
- Line 293: hideReconnectingOverlay() removes overlay → TRIGGERS Dash reconnection
- Line 295-303: Updates footer via DOM manipulation (RACES with Dash re-render)
- Line 306: Shows toast via DOM manipulation (RACES with Dash re-render)
- Line 309: Sets isUpdateFlow = false
- MISSING: No coordination with Dash reconnection state

**Why Manual Refresh Works:**
Manual refresh causes browser to:
1. Re-request page from server (gets new v2.7.17 HTML)
2. Re-initialize Dash with new version
3. Check post_update flag in /api/version (lines 541-577)
4. Show success toast via init() function (line 569) AFTER Dash initializes
5. Footer already rendered with correct version in HTML

**Solution Options:**

Option A: Force page reload after overlay removal (simplest, RECOMMENDED)
- Add window.location.reload() after line 309
- Pros: Guarantees fresh page load with new version, no race conditions
- Cons: Full page reload (brief white flash) - but expected after update
- Implementation: One line change

Option B: Wait for Dash reconnection before DOM updates
- Monitor Dash's connection state (window.dash_clientside?)
- Only update footer + show toast AFTER Dash finishes reconnecting
- Pros: No page reload
- Cons: Complex, fragile, requires Dash internals knowledge

Option C: Use post_update flag mechanism (current fallback path)
- Remove all DOM manipulation from update flow (lines 295-310)
- Let init() function (lines 541-577) handle everything on page load
- Force reload after overlay removal to trigger init() path
- Pros: Uses existing reliable code path
- Cons: Requires page reload anyway

**Recommendation:** Option A (force reload) or Option C (which is essentially Option A + code cleanup). Page reload after update is acceptable UX and eliminates all race conditions. Users EXPECT a 'fresh start' after update.

**Proposed Fix:**
`javascript
// Line 293: Remove overlay
hideReconnectingOverlay();

// IMMEDIATELY force page reload to trigger clean initialization
// This uses the post_update flag mechanism (lines 541-577)
// which reliably shows toast + updates footer after Dash initializes
window.location.reload();

// Remove lines 295-310 (DOM manipulation that races with Dash)
`

**Test Case:**
1. Start app v2.7.16
2. Check for updates (should show v2.7.17 available)
3. Click Update button
4. Wait for overlay to appear → disappear
5. VERIFY: Page reloads automatically (brief white flash acceptable)
6. VERIFY: Footer shows v2.7.17 immediately after reload
7. VERIFY: Success toast appears immediately after reload
8. VERIFY: No duplicate toasts or broken state
9. TEST ON BOTH: Fast machine AND slow machine (timing must be consistent)
title: Page not auto-refreshing after update - footer and toast only appear after manual refresh

description:
Goal: Improve performance of data loading after query update, query change, or data points slider adjustment (especially for large datasets). If loading takes >1s, show a visually themed spinner at the best UX location.

Research notes:
- Current load time can reach 5s on large datasets.
- Spinner should be non-intrusive, thematically fit the app, and only appear if load >1s.
- Optimize backend and/or frontend for faster data delivery.
- Consider async loading, caching, and UI feedback best practices.
- Do not implement yet; this bead is for future release planning.

References: User request 2026-01-27, Copilot research.
notes:
## Investigation: Staggered Loading Issue

### Problem Identified
1. Spinner works on initial load ✓
2. NO spinner during updates (slider, query changes) ✗
3. Page elements load one-by-one (jarring UX) ✗

### Root Cause
The render_tab_content callback has **cache optimization** that makes it return almost instantly when data hasn't changed. The callback flow:
1. User moves slider → triggers callback
2. Callback checks cache → finds match → returns immediately (<100ms)
3. 300ms delay prevents spinner from showing
4. BUT: Plotly charts inside the content render asynchronously afterward
5. Result: No loading indicator, then charts pop in one by one

### Why dcc.Loading Doesn't Help
- dcc.Loading only shows during *callback execution*
- Callback executes instantly (returns cached content)
- Chart rendering happens *after* callback completes (client-side)
- Loading component thinks work is done, but charts still rendering

### Potential Solutions
1. **Reduce delay_show to 100ms** - catch more updates but risk flash
2. **Add skeleton loaders** - show placeholder charts before Plotly renders
3. **Disable caching temporarily** - force callback to be slower (bad for perf)
4. **Client-side loading detection** - detect Plotly render events
5. **Pre-render charts** - generate chart JSON before returning (slowdown accepted)

### Recommendation
Need to balance performance (caching) vs UX (loading indicators). Current caching makes app FAST but loading INVISIBLE.
title: Optimize data load and add loading spinner for large datasets

   - Update document.getElementById('footer-version-text').textContent
   - Trigger Dash toast via custom event or direct DOM manipulation

close_reason: Implemented issue sorting by completion and health priority. All tests pass.
description:
## Overview
Implement intelligent ordering of issue progress bars in Sprint Tracker to match Active Work page logic: completed items at the bottom, non-completed items ordered by urgency (blocked → aging → active WIP → to do).

## Sorting Algorithm
For each issue in sprint:
1. Calculate completion bucket (0 = active, 1 = completed)
2. Calculate health priority (1-5: blocked, aging, wip, todo, completed)
3. Get issue key for stable tie-breaking

Sort by: (completion_bucket ASC, health_priority ASC, issue_key DESC)

## Priority Levels
**Completion Bucket 0 (Active Work):**
- Priority 1: Blocked - In flow_wip_statuses AND status unchanged ≥5 days
- Priority 2: Aging - In flow_wip_statuses AND status unchanged 3-4 days
- Priority 3: Active WIP - In flow_wip_statuses AND status changed ≤2 days
- Priority 4: To Do - NOT in flow_wip_statuses, NOT in flow_end_statuses

**Completion Bucket 1 (Completed):**
- Priority 5: Completed - In flow_end_statuses

## Implementation Location
**File**: visualization/sprint_charts.py

**New Functions** (add around line 230):
- _calculate_issue_health_priority() - Calculate completion bucket and health priority
- _sort_issues_by_health_priority() - Sort issues using health priority

**Modified Section** (line ~359):
Replace: issue_keys = sorted(issue_states.keys(), reverse=True)
With: issue_keys = _sort_issues_by_health_priority(...)

## Data Sources (Already Available)
- sprint_data['issue_states'] - Current issue status, points, type, summary
- status_changelog - Status change history with timestamps
- flow_end_statuses - Configuration for completion statuses
- flow_wip_statuses - Configuration for work-in-progress statuses

## Edge Cases
1. No changelog data: Falls back to issue created date
2. Issue not in flow config: Treated as 'To Do' (priority 4)
3. Multiple status changes: Uses most recent change only
4. Timezone handling: All dates converted to UTC
5. Completed items stay at bottom: Even if they were blocked before completion

## Testing Strategy
**Unit Tests** (new file: tests/unit/visualization/test_sprint_charts_sorting.py):
- Test blocked issue sorts before aging
- Test aging issue sorts before active WIP
- Test completed issues sort to bottom
- Test tie-breaker by issue key (descending)
- Test fallback when no changelog exists
- Test timezone handling
- Test empty flow configuration

## Success Criteria
✓ Completed issues always appear at bottom
✓ Blocked issues (5+ days) appear at top
✓ Sorting matches Active Work page logic
✓ No new configuration required
✓ No performance degradation
✓ All tests pass
title: Sprint Tracker: Order issues by completion and health priority

close_reason: Investigation complete - root cause was missing postprocess trigger (now fixed), not SQLite refactoring. Delta fetch validated working.
description:
User reports changelog being fetched repeatedly during metrics calculation. Need to verify:
1. sync_jira_scope_and_data fetches issues THEN changelog ONCE
2. historical_calculator pre-fetches changelog if not in DB  
3. weekly_calculator just checks DB, never fetches
4. Delta fetch optimization (Update Data should fetch only changed issues)

CRITICAL: Our 'fixes' in historical_calculator.py and weekly_calculator.py may be correct but there might be a bug introduced during SQLite refactoring that breaks the fetch-before-calculation invariant.
notes:
FINDINGS:

**Expected Flow (Update Data)**:
1. callbacks/settings/data_update.py → _start_background_sync()
2. data/jira/scope_sync.py → sync_jira_scope_and_data():
   - Fetches JIRA issues (with delta optimization)
   - Fetches changelog ONCE (line 229)
   - Calculates scope
   - Saves to database
3. callbacks/settings/metrics.py → auto_calculate_metrics_after_fetch()
4. data/metrics/historical_calculator.py → calculate_metrics_for_last_n_weeks():
   - Pre-fetches changelog if not in DB (lines 72-116)
   - Loops through weeks calling weekly_calculator
5. data/metrics/weekly_calculator.py → calculate_and_save_weekly_metrics():
   - Just checks DB for changelog (lines 162-178)
   - NEVER fetches

**Our Fixes**:
- Added pre-fetch in historical_calculator.py (CORRECT)
- Removed fetch from weekly_calculator.py (CORRECT)

**Root Cause Hypothesis**:
The fetch is happening at step 2 (sync_jira_scope_and_data) which is CORRECT. User's complaint suggests:
1. Maybe changelog fetch in sync_jira_scope_and_data is slow/buggy
2. Maybe SQLite refactoring broke database operations
3. Maybe delta fetch isn't working (fetching all 324 issues every time)

**Next Steps**:
1. Test Update Data and examine logs
2. Verify changelog is saved to database correctly
3. Verify delta fetch works (only changed issues)
4. Check if SQLite refactoring broke get_changelog_entries or similar methods
title: INVESTIGATION: Review data fetch flow - changelog and delta fetch may have been broken by SQLite refactoring

close_reason: Vendor dependencies download script and tracking implemented. Files: download_vendor_dependencies.py, vendor_dependencies.txt
description:
Research complete. Implementation needed to eliminate external dependencies for offline operation.

## Current State

### External Dependencies in Dash App (app.py)
Currently loading from CDNs:
- **Font Awesome 6.5.1** (cdnjs.cloudflare.com):
  - fontawesome.min.css
  - solid.min.css
  - brands.min.css
- **CodeMirror 5.65.16** (cdnjs.cloudflare.com):
  - codemirror.min.css
  - codemirror.min.js
  - mode/sql/sql.min.js

Found in: app.py external_stylesheets and external_scripts (lines 396-414)

### Report Dependencies (ALREADY HANDLED)
Report generation is already offline-capable:
- Bootstrap, Font Awesome, Chart.js, annotation plugin
- Downloaded to report_assets/ by download_report_dependencies.py
- Embedded in HTML by report_assets_embedder.py
- Versions tracked in report_dependencies.txt

### Build Process
- PyInstaller spec (build/app.spec) includes assets/ and report_assets/
- Build process handled by build/build.ps1
- No changes needed if files placed in correct locations

### Content Security Policy
Current CSP (app.py lines 450-457) whitelists:
- cdnjs.cloudflare.com
- cdn.jsdelivr.net
- fonts.googleapis.com (defensive, not actually used)
- fonts.gstatic.com (defensive, not actually used)

After refactoring, CSP needs updating to remove external domains.

## Implementation Plan

### 1. Create Download Script
Create download_dash_dependencies.py (similar to download_report_dependencies.py):
- Download Font Awesome 6.5.1 files to assets/vendor/fontawesome/
- Download CodeMirror 5.65.16 files to assets/vendor/codemirror/
- Track versions in new dash_dependencies.txt file
- Include version update capability

### 2. File Organization
Proposed structure:
`
assets/
  vendor/           # NEW directory for third-party libraries
    fontawesome/
      fontawesome.min.css
      solid.min.css
      brands.min.css
      webfonts/     # Font files (woff2, ttf)
    codemirror/
      codemirror.min.css
      codemirror.min.js
      mode/
        sql/
          sql.min.js
`

### 3. Update app.py
Replace CDN URLs in external_stylesheets and external_scripts with local paths:
- Change from: https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/...
- Change to: /assets/vendor/...
- Update both stylesheets (lines 400-403) and scripts (lines 411-412)

### 4. Update Content Security Policy
Remove CDN whitelisting from CSP (lines 450-457):
- Remove: https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com
- Remove: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net
- Keep 'self' for local assets
- Keep data: for base64 embedded fonts
- Remove fonts.googleapis.com and fonts.gstatic.com (not used)

### 5. Build Process Integration
- Verify assets/vendor/ is included in PyInstaller bundle (should be automatic via existing assets/ inclusion)
- Test frozen executable can load local assets
- Update build documentation if needed

### 6. Version Management
Create dash_dependencies.txt with format:
`
# Dash App Dependencies for Offline Operation
font-awesome==6.5.1
codemirror==5.65.16
`

### 7. Testing Requirements
- Test Dash app loads with local assets
- Verify CodeMirror syntax highlighting works for JQL editor
- Test Font Awesome icons render correctly
- Test frozen executable (PyInstaller bundle)
- Verify app works without internet connection
- Check browser console for 404 errors or external requests

### 8. Documentation Updates
- Update README.md with offline capability info
- Document version update process
- Add section about dependency management

## Benefits
- **True offline operation**: No internet required for app functionality
- **Faster loading**: No CDN latency, assets loaded from disk
- **Security**: Eliminates external script injection risk (Font Awesome checkout popup issue)
- **Consistency**: CSP can be locked down to 'self' only for scripts/styles
- **Privacy**: No external requests that could leak usage data
- **Reliability**: Not dependent on CDN availability or company network policies

## Edge Cases
- Font Awesome fonts: Need all webfont files (woff2, ttf) for cross-browser support
- CodeMirror modes: Currently only using sql.min.js, but structure should support adding more modes
- Version updates: Script should allow updating to newer versions easily
- Build size: Local assets add ~1-2MB to bundle (acceptable tradeoff)

## Files to Create/Modify
**Create:**
- download_dash_dependencies.py
- dash_dependencies.txt
- assets/vendor/ directory structure

**Modify:**
- app.py (external_stylesheets, external_scripts, CSP)
- build/build.ps1 (optional: add verification step)
- README.md (documentation)

## Related Systems
- Similar pattern to report_dependencies.txt and download_report_dependencies.py
- Build process already handles assets/ inclusion
- No dependency on Font Awesome kit system (using free CSS-only version)

## Priority Justification
P1 (High) - Security and reliability improvement. Enables true offline operation which is important for air-gapped environments and removes external script injection risk.
title: Download and host all CDN dependencies locally for offline operation

close_reason: Closed
description: After update completes and app restarts normally, every subsequent app launch opens a new browser window/tab instead of detecting existing tabs at http://127.0.0.1:8050. Expected: App should detect if browser tab already exists before launching new one. Actual: webbrowser.open(url, new=2) always opens new tab. Root cause: Python webbrowser module has no API to check for existing tabs. Potential solutions: 1) Add JavaScript-based heartbeat to detect connected clients, 2) Check for active WebSocket connections before launching, 3) Add startup flag --no-browser for manual control, 4) Accept current behavior as limitation of webbrowser module.
notes:
DECISION: AS DESIGNED - NOT A BUG

**User Expectation Analysis**:
When user double-clicks BurndownChart.exe:
- Expected: Browser opens immediately (visual confirmation app started)
- Actual: Browser opens ✅
- This is CORRECT behavior

**Why This Makes Sense**:
1. User has no way to know if app started without browser opening
2. Terminal window might be hidden/minimized
3. System tray icon might not be noticed immediately
4. Opening browser = clear visual feedback that app is running

**Update Scenario is Different**:
- User KNOWS app is running (existing tab open)
- update_reconnect.js handles reconnection automatically
- Flag prevents duplicate tab (correct)

**Multiple Tabs from Repeated Launches**:
- If user launches exe multiple times = multiple tabs is expected
- User controls this by not double-clicking multiple times
- System tray 'Open in Browser' available if tab is closed

**Conclusion**: Current behavior is correct by design. Closing as not a bug.
title: Browser opens new window on every app launch instead of checking for existing tabs

description:
Refactor callbacks/settings.py from 3126 lines to comply with 500-line architectural guideline.

## Detailed Analysis Complete

**File**: callbacks/settings.py
**Current Size**: 3126 lines (6.25x over 500-line limit)
**Structure**: 3 helper functions + 1 register() function containing 25 callbacks

## Semantic Groups Identified

### 1. Helper Functions (Lines 1-150) → helpers/
- _normalize_show_points() - Converts various formats to boolean
- get_data_points_info() - Generates slider info text
- calculate_remaining_work_for_data_window() - Calculates current remaining work

### 2. Core Settings Management (Lines 168-640)
- update_total_points_calculation() - Calculate total points from inputs
- update_remaining_points_formula() - Display formula with coefficient
- reload_settings_after_import_or_switch() - Load settings from DB
- sync_ui_inputs_with_settings() - Sync UI with settings store
- update_and_save_settings() - Save settings changes to DB

### 3. Data Update & JIRA Integration (Lines 640-1836)
- handle_unified_data_update() - Massive unified data update callback (~1000 lines!)
  - Contains background_sync() nested function
- auto_calculate_metrics_after_fetch() - Auto metrics after data fetch (~200 lines)
  - Contains metrics_progress_callback() and background_metrics() nested functions
- calculate_jira_project_scope() - Calculate scope from JIRA (~200 lines)

### 4. JQL Query Profile Management (Lines 2016-2726)
- handle_save_query_modal() - Open/close save modal
- save_query_profile() - Save new query profile
- sync_dropdowns_and_show_buttons() - Sync desktop/mobile selectors
- handle_delete_query_modal_cancel() - Cancel delete
- delete_query_profile() - Delete query profile
- delete_query_from_selector() - Delete from selector
- update_jql_from_profile() - Load JQL from profile
- update_query_status_message() - Update status message
- update_query_profile() - Update existing profile
- load_default_query() - Load default query
- test_jql_query_validity() - Test JQL query

### 5. Parameter Panel UI (Lines 3002-3529)
- toggle_parameter_panel() - Expand/collapse panel
- update_parameter_summary() - Update summary display
- update_data_points_slider_marks() - Dynamic slider marks
- reload_scope_after_metrics() - Reload scope after calc
- update_remaining_work_on_data_points_change() - Update on slider change

## Proposed File Structure

`

├── __init__.py                  # Register all callbacks (~50 lines)

`

- configuration (constants, logger)
- data (calculate_total_points)
- data.persistence (load/save functions)
- data.jira (JIRA operations)
- data.profile_manager
- ui.parameter_panel

1. Create callbacks/settings/ directory
2. Extract helpers to helpers.py
3. Split register() function into focused modules
4. Create __init__.py to register all callbacks
5. Run tests and get_errors to verify
6. Update imports in dependent files if needed

description:
Create a dedicated Roadmap/Timeline tab with gantt chart visualization for planning and tracking epics and high-level deliverables. This feature enables users to visualize project timelines, track epic progress, and manage milestones separately from sprint-level burndown tracking.

**Key Features:**
- Dedicated tab for roadmap view (separate from sprint burndown)
- Gantt chart showing epics/initiatives with start and end dates
- Configurable JIRA issue types (Epic, Initiative, Release, etc.)
- Flexible date field mapping (target dates, fixVersions, or fallback to created/resolution)
- Vertical milestone markers at configurable dates
- Handle ongoing work (no end date = extend to horizon)
- Separate JQL query configuration from main burndown query
- Color coding by issue type, status, or project
- Mobile-first responsive design

**Technical Approach:**
- Reuse existing field mapping system (similar to DORA/Flow metrics)
- Leverage SQLite persistence with separate roadmap query namespace
- Plotly Gantt chart visualization
- Follow established tab configuration pattern (TAB_CONFIG registry)
- ~8-12 days implementation effort

**Use Cases:**
- Product managers tracking epic delivery timelines
- Release planning with milestone markers
- Portfolio view of multiple initiatives
- Stakeholder communication with high-level roadmap
- Dependency visualization across epics

**Prerequisites:**
- JIRA epics or similar issue types with date fields (or use created/resolved as fallback)
- Optional: Custom fields for target start/end dates
- Existing burndown chart profile and JIRA connection
notes:
## Technical Analysis

### Architecture Overview

**Current System Structure:**
- **Tab System:** TAB_CONFIG registry in ui/tabs.py defines all tabs (currently 7 tabs)
- **Query Management:** data/query_manager.py handles profile-based queries with SQLite persistence
- **JIRA API:** data/jira_simple.py with field mapping support via field_mappings config
- **Database:** SQLite backend (profiles/burndown.db) with 12 tables
- **Visualization:** Plotly charts in visualization/ package
- **Tab Navigation:** Mobile + desktop responsive with dbc.Tabs component

### Roadmap/Timeline Requirements Analysis

**1. JIRA Data Requirements:**
- **Issue Types:** Configurable (Epic, Initiative, Release, etc.)
- **Date Fields:** 
  - Primary: Target Start/End dates (customfield_XXXXX)
  - Fallback: created/resolutiondate (standard fields)
  - Support: fixVersions.releaseDate for version-based roadmaps
- **Field Mapping:** Leverage existing field_mappings system (FR-006 through FR-010)
- **Query:** Separate JQL query distinct from current burndown query

**2. Data Model Considerations:**
- **Unlimited End Dates:** Handle NULL or ongoing items (no resolution date)
- **Visualization:** Gantt chart needs start date, end date (or current date as proxy)
- **Milestones:** Vertical lines at configurable dates (similar to deadline markers)
- **Time Range:** Calculate min/max dates from dataset + configurable padding

**3. UI/UX Design:**
- **New Tab:** Add to TAB_CONFIG with order=7 (after Weekly Data)
  - id: 'tab-roadmap' or 'tab-timeline'
  - icon: 'fa-calendar-alt' or 'fa-road'
  - requires_data: True
- **Query Configuration:** Dedicated settings for roadmap JQL query
- **Date Field Mapping:** UI to map issue type date fields (similar to DORA field mapping)
- **Milestone Configuration:** Input for milestone dates with labels

**4. Visualization Strategy:**
- **Library:** Plotly Gantt chart (plotly.figure_factory.create_gantt)
- **Alternative:** Custom Plotly.graph_objects with horizontal bars
- **Features Needed:**
  - Color coding by issue type or status
  - Hover info: issue key, summary, dates
  - Vertical milestone lines (similar to add_deadline_marker)
  - Today marker (vertical line showing current date)
  - Zoom/pan for long timelines
  - Ongoing items: extend to current date or future cutoff

**5. Data Flow Pattern:**
`
User configures roadmap query (JQL + issue types + date fields)
  ↓
Separate JIRA API call (fetch_jira_issues with roadmap config)
  ↓
Store in SQLite (separate table or query namespace)
  ↓
Transform to Gantt format (start_date, end_date, task_name)
  ↓
Render Plotly Gantt chart in tab-roadmap content
  ↓
Add milestone overlays (vertical lines)
`

### Implementation Approach

**Phase 1: Configuration & Data (3-4 days)**
- Add roadmap_config to app_settings.json:
  - roadmap_jql: str (JQL query for roadmap items)
  - roadmap_issue_types: List[str] (Epic, Initiative, etc.)
  - roadmap_start_field: str (customfield_XXXXX or 'created')
  - roadmap_end_field: str (customfield_XXXXX or 'resolutiondate')
  - roadmap_milestones: List[{date: str, label: str}]
- Update data/query_manager.py to support roadmap query type
- Create data/roadmap_fetcher.py for JIRA data retrieval
- Add SQLite table or use existing jira_issues with roadmap query_id

**Phase 2: Visualization (2-3 days)**
- Create visualization/roadmap_chart.py
- Implement create_roadmap_gantt_chart():
  - Transform JIRA issues to Gantt format
  - Handle unlimited end dates (use today or max_date)
  - Add milestone vertical lines
  - Color code by issue type/status
- Handle edge cases (single-day items, overlapping items)

**Phase 3: UI Integration (2-3 days)**
- Add tab to TAB_CONFIG in ui/tabs.py
- Create ui/roadmap_tab.py for layout
- Create callbacks/roadmap.py for data updates
- Add roadmap settings to settings panel (accordion or tab)
- Mobile-first responsive design

**Phase 4: Testing & Polish (1-2 days)**
- Unit tests for Gantt chart generation
- Integration tests for JIRA data fetch
- Edge case testing (no data, all ongoing, etc.)
- Performance testing (100+ items)

### Technical Challenges & Solutions

**Challenge 1: Unlimited End Dates**
- **Solution:** Use today + configurable horizon (e.g., 6 months) or max(all_end_dates) + buffer

**Challenge 2: Separate Query Management**
- **Solution:** Reuse existing query_manager.py pattern, add roadmap query type

**Challenge 3: Gantt Chart Performance**
- **Solution:** Limit to 200 items, provide filtering by issue type/project

**Challenge 4: Date Field Flexibility**
- **Solution:** Field mapping UI similar to DORA/Flow metrics (callbacks/field_mapping.py pattern)

### Files to Modify/Create

**New Files:**
- data/roadmap_fetcher.py (JIRA query + transform)
- visualization/roadmap_chart.py (Gantt chart generation)
- ui/roadmap_tab.py (tab layout)
- callbacks/roadmap.py (callbacks for data updates)
- tests/unit/visualization/test_roadmap_chart.py
- tests/unit/callbacks/test_roadmap.py

**Modified Files:**
- ui/tabs.py (add TAB_CONFIG entry)
- configuration/settings.py (add roadmap help texts)
- data/persistence/sqlite_backend.py (if new table needed)
- ui/improved_settings_panel.py (add roadmap settings)
- callbacks/__init__.py (register roadmap callbacks)

### Dependencies

**Existing Patterns to Reuse:**
- Field mapping: callbacks/field_mapping.py, data/field_mapper.py
- Query management: data/query_manager.py
- Deadline markers: visualization/charts.py::add_deadline_marker()
- Tab structure: ui/tabs.py::TAB_CONFIG
- Mobile navigation: ui/mobile_navigation.py

**External Libraries:**
- plotly (already used): Gantt chart support
- No new dependencies required

### Success Criteria

- [ ] User can configure separate JQL query for roadmap
- [ ] User can map issue type date fields (start/end)
- [ ] Gantt chart displays with correct timeline
- [ ] Unlimited end dates handled gracefully
- [ ] Milestone lines appear at configured dates
- [ ] Responsive on mobile and desktop
- [ ] Performance <2s for 100 items
- [ ] Works with standard JIRA fields (fallback to created/resolutiondate)

### Research Recommendations

1. **Plotly Gantt API:** Test plotly.figure_factory.create_gantt vs custom bars
2. **Apache Kafka JIRA:** Check if Epics have target start/end custom fields
3. **Field Detection:** Can field_detector.py auto-detect date fields in Epics?
4. **User Preferences:** Survey users on preferred name: Roadmap vs Timeline
5. **Date Horizon:** Research common roadmap time ranges (3/6/12 months?)

### Estimated Effort

- **Total:** 8-12 days (64-96 hours)
- **Phase 1:** 3-4 days
- **Phase 2:** 2-3 days
- **Phase 3:** 2-3 days
- **Phase 4:** 1-2 days

**Complexity:** Medium-High (requires coordination across multiple layers)
**Risk:** Low (reuses existing patterns extensively)



## Interactive Roadmap Requirements (User-Editable Gantt Chart)

### Updated Feature Scope

**CRITICAL CHANGE:** Roadmap must be fully interactive with drag-and-drop editing capabilities, NOT static Plotly visualization.

**User Interactions Required:**
1. **Drag Blocks Vertically:** Reorder items in timeline
2. **Drag Blocks Horizontally:** Change start/end dates
3. **Resize Blocks:** Adjust target start/end dates by dragging edges
4. **Edit Names:** Click to edit issue summary text inline
5. **Local Persistence:** All changes saved to SQLite, NEVER synced to JIRA
6. **Override Warning:** Re-fetching JIRA data will overwrite local changes

### Interactive Timeline Library Options

**Option 1: vis-timeline (Recommended)**
- **Library:** vis-timeline (formerly vis.js timeline)
- **npm:** vis-timeline (MIT license)
- **Features:** Full drag-and-drop, resize, edit, zoom, pan
- **Dash Integration:** Custom Dash component wrapper or iframe + clientside callbacks
- **Data Format:** JSON timeline items with start/end dates
- **Pros:** Battle-tested, feature-complete, great documentation
- **Cons:** Requires custom Dash component or iframe integration
- **Example:** https://visjs.github.io/vis-timeline/examples/

**Option 2: react-beautiful-dnd + Custom Timeline**
- **Library:** react-beautiful-dnd (Atlassian)
- **npm:** react-beautiful-dnd
- **Features:** Smooth drag-and-drop, accessibility
- **Dash Integration:** Create custom Dash component with React
- **Pros:** React-based (Dash uses React), smooth animations
- **Cons:** Requires custom timeline rendering logic
- **Complexity:** High (custom timeline implementation)

**Option 3: FullCalendar Timeline View**
- **Library:** FullCalendar (premium timeline addon)
- **License:** Commercial license required for timeline
- **Features:** Professional timeline with resource management
- **Dash Integration:** iframe or custom component
- **Pros:** Professional, feature-rich
- **Cons:** Paid license, heavyweight

**Option 4: dhtmlxGantt**
- **Library:** dhtmlxGantt
- **License:** GPL (free) or Commercial
- **Features:** Full gantt chart with dependencies, editing
- **Dash Integration:** iframe or custom component
- **Pros:** Purpose-built for gantt charts
- **Cons:** License complexity, large library

**RECOMMENDATION: vis-timeline (Option 1)**
- Open source (MIT)
- Perfect feature match (drag, resize, edit)
- Proven in production
- Integration: Custom Dash component or iframe + postMessage

### Integration Architecture: vis-timeline + Dash

**Approach A: Custom Dash Component (Recommended)**
- Create custom React component wrapping vis-timeline
- Package as Dash component library
- Full Dash integration with callbacks
- **Effort:** 3-4 days for component development
- **Reference:** dash-bootstrap-components pattern

**Approach B: iframe + postMessage (Faster)**
- Serve vis-timeline in iframe (separate HTML)
- Use postMessage for JS ↔ Dash communication
- Clientside callbacks to sync data
- **Effort:** 1-2 days for prototype
- **Trade-off:** Less elegant but faster to implement

**Approach C: Dash Mantine Components Timeline (Alternative)**
- **Library:** dash-mantine-components (has Timeline component)
- **Limitation:** Timeline is display-only, not drag-and-drop gantt
- **Verdict:** Not suitable for roadmap requirements

### Data Flow: Two-Layer Persistence

**Layer 1: JIRA Source of Truth (Read-Only)**
`
JIRA API → SQLite jira_issues table
- Original issue data from JIRA
- Fields: issue_key, summary, start_date, end_date, issue_type, status
- Re-fetch overwrites this layer
`

**Layer 2: Local Overrides (User Edits)**
`
User Edits → SQLite roadmap_overrides table
- Tracks local modifications
- Fields: issue_key, custom_summary, custom_start_date, custom_end_date, custom_order
- Persists across sessions
- NEVER synced to JIRA
`

**Merge Logic (Display):**
`python
def get_roadmap_items():
    jira_items = backend.get_roadmap_issues()  # From jira_issues
    overrides = backend.get_roadmap_overrides()  # From roadmap_overrides
    
    for item in jira_items:
        if item['key'] in overrides:
            # Apply local overrides
            item['summary'] = overrides[item['key']].get('custom_summary', item['summary'])
            item['start_date'] = overrides[item['key']].get('custom_start_date', item['start_date'])
            item['end_date'] = overrides[item['key']].get('custom_end_date', item['end_date'])
            item['order'] = overrides[item['key']].get('custom_order', item['order'])
    
    return sorted(jira_items, key=lambda x: x['order'])
`

### Database Schema (New Table)

`sql
CREATE TABLE roadmap_overrides (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    profile_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    query_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    issue_key TEXT NOT NULL,
    custom_summary TEXT,
    custom_start_date TEXT,  -- ISO8601 format
    custom_end_date TEXT,
    custom_order INTEGER,
    modified_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    UNIQUE(profile_id, query_id, issue_key)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_roadmap_overrides_lookup 
ON roadmap_overrides(profile_id, query_id);
`

### Clientside Callback Architecture

**Pattern:** Use clientside callbacks for real-time UI updates, server callbacks for persistence

`javascript
// Clientside: Handle drag events (instant UI feedback)
window.dash_clientside.roadmap = {
    onItemDrag: function(timelineEvents) {
        // Update UI immediately
        // Return new item positions for dcc.Store
        return {
            'dragging': true,
            'items': updatedItems
        };
    },
    
    onItemResize: function(timelineEvents) {
        // Update dates immediately
        return {
            'resizing': true,
            'items': updatedItems
        };
    }
};
`

`python
# Server: Persist changes to SQLite
@callback(
    Output('roadmap-save-status', 'data'),
    Input('roadmap-items-store', 'data'),
    prevent_initial_call=True
)
def save_roadmap_changes(items):
    backend.save_roadmap_overrides(items)
    return {'saved': True, 'timestamp': datetime.now()}
`

### Override Warning System

**UI Component:** Banner at top of roadmap tab
`python
dbc.Alert([
    html.I(className='fas fa-exclamation-triangle me-2'),
    'Local edits active. Re-fetching JIRA data will overwrite your changes.',
    dbc.Button('Clear Overrides', id='clear-overrides-btn', size='sm', className='ms-2')
], color='warning', id='roadmap-override-warning', dismissible=False)
`

**Behavior:**
- Show when oadmap_overrides table has records for active profile/query
- Hide when no overrides exist
- 'Clear Overrides' button deletes all overrides for current profile/query

### Technical Challenges & Solutions (Updated)

**Challenge 1: Dash ↔ JavaScript Integration**
- **Solution A (Recommended):** Custom Dash component wrapping vis-timeline
  - Create React component following dash-component-boilerplate
  - Package as dash-vis-timeline library
  - Full Dash callback integration
  - **Effort:** 3-4 days
  - **Reference:** https://github.com/plotly/dash-component-boilerplate

- **Solution B (Faster Prototype):** iframe + postMessage
  - Serve vis-timeline.html as static asset
  - Use dcc.Iframe to embed
  - Clientside callbacks + postMessage for communication
  - **Effort:** 1-2 days
  - **Trade-off:** Less elegant, harder to style consistently

**Challenge 2: Real-Time UI Updates Without Server Round-Trip**
- **Solution:** Clientside callbacks for drag/resize events
  - JavaScript handles immediate UI feedback
  - Debounced server callback for persistence (save after 1s idle)
  - Pattern: dcc.Store intermediate → debounce → server save

**Challenge 3: JIRA Re-Fetch Override Conflict**
- **Solution:** Three-way merge strategy
  - Fetch new JIRA data
  - Load local overrides
  - Detect conflicts (deleted issues, renamed issues)
  - Show conflict resolution modal with options:
    a) Keep local changes
    b) Discard local changes
    c) Merge (use JIRA data for new fields, keep local edits)

**Challenge 4: Performance with 100+ Timeline Items**
- **Solution:** vis-timeline built-in virtualization
  - Only renders visible items
  - Handles 1000+ items efficiently
  - Add filtering (by project, issue type, status)

### Files to Create/Modify (Updated)

**New Files:**
- **data/roadmap_fetcher.py** - JIRA query + merge with overrides
- **data/roadmap_overrides.py** - Override CRUD operations
- **visualization/roadmap_timeline.py** - vis-timeline integration (if custom component)
- **ui/roadmap_tab.py** - Tab layout with timeline container
- **callbacks/roadmap.py** - Server callbacks for persistence
- **callbacks/roadmap_clientside.py** - Clientside callback registrations
- **assets/roadmap_timeline.js** - vis-timeline initialization + postMessage bridge
- **assets/roadmap_timeline.html** - Standalone timeline (if iframe approach)
- **assets/vis-timeline.min.js** - vis-timeline library (vendor)
- **assets/vis-timeline.min.css** - vis-timeline styles (vendor)
- **tests/unit/data/test_roadmap_overrides.py**
- **tests/unit/callbacks/test_roadmap.py**

**Modified Files:**
- **data/persistence/sqlite_backend.py** - Add roadmap_overrides table + CRUD
- **ui/tabs.py** - Add TAB_CONFIG entry for roadmap
- **configuration/settings.py** - Add roadmap help texts
- **ui/improved_settings_panel.py** - Add roadmap query configuration
- **callbacks/__init__.py** - Register roadmap callbacks

**SQL Migration:**
- **migrations/add_roadmap_overrides_table.sql** - Create roadmap_overrides table

### Estimated Effort (Revised)

**With Custom Dash Component (Recommended):**
- **Phase 0: Component Development:** 3-4 days (dash-vis-timeline wrapper)
- **Phase 1: Configuration & Data:** 3-4 days (override persistence, merge logic)
- **Phase 2: Integration:** 2-3 days (callbacks, clientside JS)
- **Phase 3: UI & Testing:** 2-3 days (tab layout, override warning, tests)
- **Total:** 10-14 days (80-112 hours)

**With iframe Approach (Faster Prototype):**
- **Phase 1: Configuration & Data:** 3-4 days
- **Phase 2: iframe Integration:** 1-2 days (static HTML + postMessage)
- **Phase 3: UI & Testing:** 2-3 days
- **Total:** 6-9 days (48-72 hours)

**Complexity:** High (custom component or iframe + complex data sync)
**Risk:** Medium (depends on Dash ↔ JavaScript integration approach)

### Success Criteria (Updated)

- [ ] User can drag timeline blocks vertically to reorder
- [ ] User can drag timeline blocks horizontally to change dates
- [ ] User can resize blocks by dragging edges
- [ ] User can click to edit issue summary inline
- [ ] All edits persist to SQLite roadmap_overrides table
- [ ] Edits survive page refresh and app restart
- [ ] Override warning banner shows when local edits exist
- [ ] Re-fetching JIRA data overwrites with clear warning
- [ ] Clear Overrides button removes all local changes
- [ ] Performance <2s for 100 items
- [ ] Responsive on desktop (mobile not required for roadmap)

### Research Tasks (Updated)

1. **vis-timeline Integration POC:** Create minimal Dash app with vis-timeline iframe
2. **Custom Component Feasibility:** Investigate dash-component-boilerplate for React wrapper
3. **postMessage Protocol:** Design message format for timeline ↔ Dash communication
4. **Override Conflict UI:** Design modal for JIRA re-fetch conflict resolution
5. **Performance Testing:** Test vis-timeline with 500+ items in browser

### Alternative: Dash AG Grid Gantt View

**Note:** dash-ag-grid (v31.0.0+) supports gantt chart rendering but is primarily for tabular data with timeline view, not drag-and-drop scheduling. Not suitable for interactive roadmap.

### Recommendation Summary

**For MVP:** Use **iframe + vis-timeline + postMessage** approach (6-9 days)
- Faster to implement
- Proven library with all required features
- Can migrate to custom component later if needed

**For Production:** Create **custom dash-vis-timeline component** (10-14 days)
- Better Dash integration
- Consistent styling
- Full callback support
- Reusable for future projects



## fixVersions Support (Additional Date Source)

JIRA fixVersions provide structured release planning data with built-in date fields:

**fixVersions Data Structure:**
- fixVersions.name: Release name (e.g., 'v2.1.0', 'Q1 2026')
- fixVersions.startDate: Planned release start date (ISO8601)
- fixVersions.releaseDate: Target release date (ISO8601)
- fixVersions.released: Boolean flag (completed releases)
- fixVersions.archived: Boolean flag (historical releases)

**Integration Strategy:**
1. Primary Data Source: Use fixVersions dates when available
2. Fallback Hierarchy:
   - fixVersions.startDate/releaseDate (preferred)
   - Custom target date fields (customfield_XXXXX)
   - Created/resolutionDate (last resort)

**Roadmap Display Logic:**
- Issue with fixVersions → Use fixVersion start/release dates as timeline block
- Multiple fixVersions → Show as separate timeline blocks OR merge date range
- No fixVersions → Fall back to custom fields or created/resolved dates

**Database Schema Update:**
Add to roadmap_overrides table:
- fix_version_name TEXT (track which version is overridden)
- original_start_date TEXT (preserve JIRA original for conflict detection)
- original_end_date TEXT (preserve JIRA original for conflict detection)

**UI Enhancements:**
- Display fixVersion badge on timeline blocks
- Filter by fixVersion (show only items in specific release)
- Color code by fixVersion (different releases = different colors)
- Release milestone markers (vertical lines at fixVersion.releaseDate)

**Example JIRA Query:**
`jql
project = MYPROJ AND fixVersion in unreleasedVersions() ORDER BY fixVersion ASC
`

**Benefits:**
- Aligns with JIRA's native release planning
- No custom field configuration needed
- Automatic release grouping
- Built-in release status (released/unreleased)

**Impact on Sub-Tasks:**
- Task 2 (Database): Add fixVersion fields to schema
- Task 3 (Data Layer): Implement fixVersion date extraction and fallback logic
- Task 4 (Integration): Display fixVersion badges on timeline items
- Task 5 (UI): Add fixVersion filter and release milestone configuration

## REFINED TECHNICAL ANALYSIS - See roadmap_analysis_refined.md

**Analysis Date**: 2026-01-29
**Key Finding**: 80-90% infrastructure reuse possible

**Major Reuse Opportunities**:
1. JIRA data fetching (100% reuse - fetch_jira_issues)
2. fixVersions handling (100% reuse - fixversion_matcher.py + DORA metrics)
3. Date field detection (90% reuse - field_detector.py)
4. Field mapping UI (95% reuse - field_mapping_modal.py)
5. Query management (100% reuse - query_manager.py + SQLite backend)

**Effort Reduction**:
- Original estimate: 8-12 days
- Refined estimate: 6-9 days (25-33% reduction)
- Primary new work: vis-timeline integration (iframe + postMessage)

**Implementation Strategy**:
- Use existing fetch_jira_issues() with roadmap-specific query
- Reuse fixversion_matcher.py for fixVersions.releaseDate extraction
- Follow Bug Analysis tab pattern for conditional display
- Add roadmap_overrides table for local edit persistence
- Integrate vis-timeline via iframe + postMessage (MVP approach)

**Files Modified/Created**:
- New: 6 files (~680 lines total)
- Modified: 8 files (~217 lines added)
- Reuse: 80-90% of core infrastructure

**Detailed analysis document**: roadmap_analysis_refined.md (9-page technical breakdown)

description:
Implement conflict resolution when re-fetching JIRA data with active local overrides.

**Scenarios:**
1. **New Issues:** Add to roadmap (no conflict)
2. **Deleted Issues:** Remove from JIRA, keep override as orphan or delete
3. **Modified Issues:** JIRA data changed, local override exists
4. **Renamed Issues:** Issue key unchanged but summary changed

**Conflict Detection:**
`python
def detect_conflicts(jira_issues, overrides) -> List[Conflict]:
    '''Compare JIRA data with local overrides'''
    conflicts = []
    for issue_key in overrides:
        if issue_key not in jira_issues:
            conflicts.append(Conflict('deleted', issue_key))
        elif jira_issues[issue_key].summary != overrides[issue_key].original_summary:
            conflicts.append(Conflict('modified', issue_key))
    return conflicts
`

**Resolution Modal:**
- Show list of conflicts
- Options per conflict:
  - Keep local changes
  - Use JIRA data
  - Delete override
- Batch action: Apply to all

**Files:**
- data/roadmap_conflict_resolver.py (new)
- ui/roadmap_conflict_modal.py (new)
- callbacks/roadmap_conflict.py (new)

**Effort:** 1-2 days
title: Conflict Resolution: JIRA re-fetch with override handling

close_reason: Implemented linear progression weights (days_completed/5) for blending algorithm. Friday now uses 80/20 blend instead of 100% actual. All tests pass.
description:
Currently the app switches from blended to actual data when Friday starts. This is incorrect because people can still close/open issues during Friday. Should use blended data for entire Friday and switch to actual data starting Saturday/Sunday.

=== MATHEMATICAL RESEARCH FINDINGS ===

PROBLEM WITH CURRENT WEIGHTS:
Current: Mon=0.0, Tue=0.2, Wed=0.5, Thu=0.8, Fri=1.0
Issues:
1. Wednesday over-weights actual (50% after only 40% of week)
2. Thursday over-weights actual (80% after only 60% of week)
3. Friday using 100% actual is premature (work still happening)
4. Weights are arbitrary, not based on mathematical principle

PROPOSED: LINEAR PROGRESSION (Mathematically Sound)
Formula: weight = days_completed / 5

New Weights:
- Monday: 0.0 (0/5 = 0% actual, 100% forecast) - unchanged
- Tuesday: 0.2 (1/5 = 20% actual, 80% forecast) - unchanged
- Wednesday: 0.4 (2/5 = 40% actual, 60% forecast) - changed from 0.5
- Thursday: 0.6 (3/5 = 60% actual, 40% forecast) - changed from 0.8
- Friday: 0.8 (4/5 = 80% actual, 20% forecast) - changed from 1.0
- Saturday: 1.0 (5/5 = 100% actual, 0% forecast) - unchanged
- Sunday: 1.0 (work week complete) - unchanged

MATHEMATICAL JUSTIFICATION:
1. Each workday contributes exactly 20% (1/5) to confidence
2. Weight proportional to elapsed time in work week
3. No arbitrary jumps or magic numbers
4. Linear progression = smooth, predictable metrics
5. Reflects that Friday is still an active work day

COMPARISON (Example: 10 actual, 20 forecast):
Day | Current | New | Impact
Mon | 20.0    | 20.0 | No change
Tue | 18.0    | 18.0 | No change
Wed | 15.0    | 16.0 | More conservative (less weight on incomplete actual)
Thu | 12.0    | 14.0 | More conservative (smoother progression)
Fri | 10.0    | 16.0 | SOLVES ISSUE (blended data continues!)
Sat | 10.0    | 10.0 | No change

FILES TO UPDATE:
1. data/metrics/blending.py:
   - Line 16: 'Friday-Sunday (4-6): 1.0' → 'Friday (4): 0.8, Saturday-Sunday (5-6): 1.0'
   - Line 33: WEEKDAY_WEIGHTS[2] = 0.5 → 0.4
   - Line 34: WEEKDAY_WEIGHTS[3] = 0.8 → 0.6
   - Line 35: WEEKDAY_WEIGHTS[4] = 1.0 → 0.8
   - Line 179: 'Blending active Mon-Thu' → 'Mon-Fri'
   - Line 206: '(Fri-Sun, or weight=1.0)' → '(Sat-Sun, or weight=1.0)'
   - Line 7: 'Friday-Sunday' → 'Saturday-Sunday'
   - Update docstring to explain linear progression formula

2. ui/metric_cards.py:
   - Line 1313: Comment 'Weekend (Fri-Sun)' → 'Weekend (Sat-Sun)'

3. tests/unit/data/test_metrics_blending.py:
   - Line 45-47: test_wednesday_weight() - Update from 0.5 to 0.4
   - Line 52-54: test_thursday_weight() - Update from 0.8 to 0.6
   - Line 59-62: test_friday_weight() - Update from 1.0 to 0.8
   - Line 111-114: test_wednesday_blend_50_50() - Update calculation and name
   - Line 120-124: test_thursday_blend_80_20() - Update calculation and name
   - Line 128-131: test_friday_blend_pure_actual() - Rename and update to expect blended
   - Line 252-258: test_metadata_friday_no_blending() - Update to expect is_blended=True
   - Line 294-296: test_metadata_values_wednesday() - Update percentages
   - Line 300-307: test_description_friday_no_blend() - Rename and update expectations
   - Line 312-327: test_monday_to_friday_progression() - Update expected values array

4. Documentation:
   - docs/metrics_correlation_guide.md: Update weights (lines 228, 234, 235)
   - docs/flow_metrics.md: Update weights and examples (line 54-66)
   - docs/dora_metrics.md: Update weights (line 51-56)
   - Update all docs to explain linear progression formula

BENEFITS:
✓ Mathematically sound (proportional to work week elapsed)
✓ Solves original issue (Friday remains blended)
✓ Smoother progression (equal 20% increments)
✓ More conservative (doesn't over-weight incomplete data)
✓ Transparent and explainable to users
✓ Aligns with empirical work completion patterns

USER-VISIBLE CHANGES:
- Friday cards: 'Based on 80% actual, 20% forecast (Friday)' instead of 'Using actual data (Friday)'
- Wednesday cards: '40% actual, 60% forecast' instead of '50% / 50%'
- Thursday cards: '60% actual, 40% forecast' instead of '80% / 20%'
- Saturday/Sunday: Continue showing 'Using actual data' (100% actual)
title: Fix blended calculation to use actual data from Saturday, not Friday

close_reason: Implementation complete, all tests passing
description:
Implement progressive blending function f(forecast, actual) for current week metrics to eliminate Monday reliability drop and smooth weekly progression. Full feature implementation using fixed Mon-Fri schedule.

## Problem
- Rolling averages include incomplete current week with 40% weight
- Monday shows 0 completions → 25% drop in metrics (8.4 vs 11.2 items/week)
- Creates 'sawtooth pattern' - metrics artificially decline Mon and recover by Fri
- Unreliable Monday-Thursday numbers erode trust in system
- Downstream calculations (PERT, velocity projections) inherit instability

## Solution: Progressive Blending f(forecast, actual)
Implement weighted blending that combines forecast (from prior 4 weeks) with actual current week value, weighted by day of week. Fixed Mon-Fri schedule for initial implementation.

## Blending Formula
\\\python
# Day-of-week detection using datetime.now().weekday()
# Returns: 0=Monday, 1=Tuesday, 2=Wednesday, 3=Thursday, 4=Friday, 5=Saturday, 6=Sunday

# Actual weight by day (0-6)
weights = {
    0: 0.0,   # Monday: 0% actual, 100% forecast
    1: 0.2,   # Tuesday: 20% actual, 80% forecast
    2: 0.5,   # Wednesday: 50% actual, 50% forecast
    3: 0.8,   # Thursday: 80% actual, 20% forecast
    4: 1.0,   # Friday: 100% actual, 0% forecast
    5: 1.0,   # Saturday: 100% actual (work week complete)
    6: 1.0    # Sunday: 100% actual (work week complete)
}

actual_weight = weights[current_weekday]
forecast_weight = 1.0 - actual_weight
blended_value = (actual * actual_weight) + (forecast * forecast_weight)
\\\

## Day-of-Week Timing Specification

**Fixed Mon-Fri schedule**:
- **Monday 0:00 - 23:59**: 0% actual, 100% forecast (weekday=0)
- **Tuesday 0:00 - 23:59**: 20% actual, 80% forecast (weekday=1)
- **Wednesday 0:00 - 23:59**: 50% actual, 50% forecast (weekday=2)
- **Thursday 0:00 - 23:59**: 80% actual, 20% forecast (weekday=3)
- **Friday 0:00 - 23:59**: 100% actual, 0% forecast (weekday=4)
- **Saturday 0:00 - 23:59**: 100% actual, 0% forecast (weekday=5)
- **Sunday 0:00 - 23:59**: 100% actual, 0% forecast (weekday=6)

**Time Detection**: Use \datetime.now().weekday()\ (returns 0-6, Monday=0)

**Rationale**: Saturday/Sunday use 100% actual because work week (Mon-Fri) is complete. No blending needed for weekend days.

## Week Progression Example (Mon-Fri team)

**Scenario**: Team averaging 11.5 items/week, current week has variable actuals

| Day | Current Week Actual | Forecast | Blended Value | Result |
|-----|---------------------|----------|---------------|--------|
| **Monday** | 0 items | 11.5 | (0 × 0%) + (11.5 × 100%) | **11.5** ✅ Stable! |
| **Tuesday** | 2 items | 11.5 | (2 × 20%) + (11.5 × 80%) | **9.6** |
| **Wednesday** | 5 items | 11.5 | (5 × 50%) + (11.5 × 50%) | **8.25** |
| **Thursday** | 8 items | 11.5 | (8 × 80%) + (11.5 × 20%) | **8.7** |
| **Friday** | 10 items | 11.5 | (10 × 100%) + (11.5 × 0%) | **10.0** Pure actual |

**Result**: Smooth transition, no Monday cliff (was 8.4, now 11.5), stable metrics all week.

## UI Transparency (CRITICAL - NEW)

**Display blended metrics with full breakdown on Flow/DORA metric cards**:

### Card Display Format
\\\
Flow Velocity: 9.6 items/week 📊

Breakdown:
• Blended (f): 9.6 items/week
• Forecast (x): 11.5 items/week  
• Current Week (y): 2 items (Tuesday)
• Blend Ratio: 20% actual, 80% forecast
\\\

### Components to Show
1. **Main value**: Blended result with blend indicator icon (📊)
2. **Tooltip/Expandable section**:
   - f(x,y) = Blended value
   - x = Forecast from prior 4 weeks
   - y = Current week actual
   - Percentage breakdown: 'XX% actual, YY% forecast (Day)'

### Where to Display
- **Flow Velocity card** (callbacks/dora_flow_metrics.py)
- **DORA metrics cards** (deployment frequency, lead time)
- **Team Throughput Analysis section** (explicitly requested)
- **Dashboard statistics** (if showing current week)

### UI Enhancement
- Add \ lend_indicator\ component to metric cards
- Show 'Based on X% actual, Y% forecast' in hover tooltip
- Display day of week context: '(Wednesday)'
- Use clear labeling: 'Current week (blended)'

## Implementation Files

### Core Algorithm
- **New**: \data/metrics/blending.py\
  - \calculate_current_week_blend(actual, forecast) -> float\
  - \get_weekday_weight() -> float\ (uses datetime.now().weekday())
  - \get_blend_metadata() -> Dict\ (returns x, y, ratio, day)

### Integration Points
- **Enhanced**: \callbacks/dora_flow_metrics.py\ (line ~700)
  - Replace current week aggregation with blended value
  - Add blend metadata to metric card display
  - Show f(x,y), x, y breakdown
- **Enhanced**: \data/processing.py::calculate_weekly_averages()\
  - Integrate blending for items/week calculation
  - Affects Dashboard, PERT, velocity projections
- **Enhanced**: \ui/metric_cards.py\ or metric card rendering
  - Add blend indicator icon (📊)
  - Display forecast (x), actual (y), blended (f) values
  - Show blend ratio tooltip

### UI Components
- **Enhanced**: Flow Velocity card - add breakdown display
- **Enhanced**: DORA metrics cards - add blend indicators
- **Enhanced**: Team Throughput Analysis - show f(x,y) components
- **Enhanced**: \configuration/help_content.py\ - document blending

### Testing
- **New**: \	ests/unit/data/test_metrics_blending.py\
  - Test all weekdays (Mon=0%, Tue=20%, Wed=50%, Thu=80%, Fri-Sun=100%)
  - Test weekday detection (datetime.now().weekday())
  - Test boundary conditions (zero actual, zero forecast)
  - Mock datetime.now() to test each day
- **New**: \	ests/integration/test_blending_display.py\
  - Verify Monday stability (no 25% drop)
  - Verify UI shows all 3 values (f, x, y)
  - Verify tooltip displays correctly
  - Test across week transitions

### Documentation
- **Doc**: \docs/flow_metrics.md\ (update calculation details)
- **Doc**: \docs/blending_algorithm.md\ (NEW - explain f(x,y))

## Affected Metrics
- Flow Velocity (items/week)
- Flow Time (days, median)
- Flow Efficiency (%)
- DORA Deployment Frequency
- DORA Lead Time for Changes
- All 4-week rolling averages
- Dashboard statistics
- Velocity projections
- Budget burn rate

## Migration
- Apply only to current/future weeks (no historical recalculation)
- Transparent to users (automatic improvement)
- Existing forecast calculation infrastructure reused
- Zero breaking changes to existing metrics

## Success Criteria
- ✅ Monday metrics stable (11.5 vs previous 8.4 - no >5% drop from Friday)
- ✅ Smooth progression throughout week (no sawtooth)
- ✅ UI displays f(x,y), x, and y values transparently
- ✅ Blend ratio shown: 'XX% actual, YY% forecast (Day)'
- ✅ Team Throughput Analysis shows breakdown
- ✅ Real-time feedback maintained (partial actuals visible)
- ✅ Downstream calculations inherit stability
- ✅ Zero breaking changes to existing metrics
- ✅ User trust in metrics improves

## Future Enhancement
After this feature is stable, Feature burndown-chart-17xv can add configurable working schedules to replace fixed Mon-Fri assumption.

Timeline: 1-2 days (core algorithm + UI transparency)
Complexity: Medium
Priority: 1 (High - core metrics reliability)
Dependencies: None (standalone feature)
