close_reason: Fixed: prevent_initial_call=True stops double render; synthetic epic lookup records restore name resolution. Tests pass. Branch pushed.
description:
Bug introduced with intelligent search and filter in v2.13.0. When clicking the Active Work tab, the Completed Work section initially renders correctly with full epic names (e.g., 'Error Use Cases Handling & Extended Requirements Coverage'). After 1-2 seconds, a second data load overwrites the cards showing only raw epic keys (e.g., 'A942-3673') instead of names.

ROOT CAUSE (two-pronged):

1. DOUBLE RENDER ON LOAD: Both filter_timeline and apply_or_clear_search callbacks in callbacks/active_work_timeline.py have prevent_initial_call=False. On page load: apply_or_clear_search fires and sets active-work-applied-query-store to '', which triggers filter_timeline, which overwrites the correct completed-items-section with incorrect content.

2. MISSING EPIC RECORDS IN LOOKUP: filter_timeline's _build_completed_children calls _flatten_issues(epics) which returns only child issues (no epic records). get_completed_items_by_week is called with child-only list. _group_issues_by_epic cannot find epic summaries in all_issues (epics are absent), so _get_parent_info falls back to returning the raw epic key string (e.g. 'A942-3673') instead of the resolved name.

The active-work-issues-store DOES contain epic_summary on each epic entry, but _build_completed_children discards this data by only extracting child_issues.

FIX:
1. Set prevent_initial_call=True on both filter_timeline and apply_or_clear_search to stop the spurious initial double render.
2. In _build_completed_children, build synthetic epic lookup records from the timeline data (issue_key + summary from epic_key + epic_summary) and include them alongside child issues when calling get_completed_items_by_week, so _group_issues_by_epic can resolve names.

Files: callbacks/active_work_timeline.py
title: Active Work: Completed Work cards overwrite epic names with epic keys after 1-2s

description:
## Purpose Add a Flow Analysis section to the generated HTML report that translates item path data into a self-explanatory narrative and tables for managers who will not open the app. Depends on burndown-chart-xmd7 (data layer). The report must be crystal clear with zero assumed knowledge — no tooltips, no jargon without explanation. ## Context The in-app tab (burndown-chart-ldjf, burndown-chart-h322) is for technical users who understand flow metrics. The report audience is managers who ask 'what must we do to deliver faster?' The report section must answer that question directly in plain language. ## What to build ### 1. New report template: report_assets/report_section_flow_analysis.html Jinja2 template. Conditionally rendered when metrics.flow_analysis.has_data is True. #### Template structure: Section header:

Flow Analysis

Where is time being lost in our workflow?

Executive narrative paragraph (self-explanatory, no jargon): Template variables to use: - metrics.flow_analysis.summary.total_open - metrics.flow_analysis.summary.total_completed - metrics.flow_analysis.summary.bottleneck_status - metrics.flow_analysis.summary.bottleneck_status_avg_days - metrics.flow_analysis.summary.bottleneck_status_item_count - metrics.flow_analysis.summary.avg_current_status_wait_days - metrics.flow_analysis.summary.avg_total_journey_days Example narrative to implement as template: 'Of {{ total_open }} items currently in progress, items spend an average of {{ avg_wait }} days waiting before moving to the next step. The most common delay point is {{ bottleneck_status }} ({{ item_count }} items waiting there, average {{ avg_days }} days). Completed items took an average of {{ avg_journey }} days from start to finish in the last N weeks.' If avg_current_status_wait_days > avg_total_journey_days * 0.4: Add sentence: 'More than 40% of total journey time is spent waiting — this indicates a systemic flow problem, not a capacity problem.' Open items table (top 10 worst by current status wait): Columns: Item Key | Summary | Current Status | Days Waiting | Total Journey | Parent/Epic Sort: current_status_duration_days DESC Highlight rows where is_blocked=True (red background) or is_aging=True (amber) Caption: 'Items currently waiting longest in their current status' Completed items table (top 10 worst by longest single stage): Columns: Item Key | Summary | Bottleneck Stage | Days in Stage | Total Journey | Parent/Epic Sort: longest_status_days DESC Caption: 'Recently completed items with longest stage delays' Bottleneck status summary table: Aggregate open items by current_status, show count + avg days Sort by avg days DESC, show top 5 statuses Columns: Status | Items Waiting | Avg Days Waiting Caption: 'Where items are currently waiting (open items by status)' This is the single most actionable table for managers — shows the process bottleneck directly. ### 2. New Python function: data/report/domain_metrics.py Add function: calculate_flow_analysis_metrics(weeks_count: int, week_labels: list[str]) -> dict This function: 1. Loads issues from backend (same pattern as other domain_metrics functions in same file) 2. Loads field mappings from settings 3. Calls data/flow_analysis.get_flow_analysis_data() 4. Adapts the result into template-ready dict: { 'has_data': bool, 'weeks_count': int, 'summary': { ... from get_flow_analysis_data() summary ... }, 'open_items': sorted list (top 10 by current_status_duration_days DESC), 'completed_items': sorted list (top 10 by longest_status_days DESC), 'bottleneck_by_status': [ {'status': str, 'item_count': int, 'avg_days': float}, ... # top 5 statuses by avg_days DESC, open items only ] } ### 3. Wire into report generator File: data/report/generator_metrics.py (or generator_assembly.py — check which assembles the metrics dict) Add call to calculate_flow_analysis_metrics() and assign to metrics['flow_analysis']. File: data/report/generator_assembly.py (or report_template.html) Add 'flow_analysis' to the sections list and include report_section_flow_analysis.html template. ## Plain language requirements for the report (CRITICAL) The report is read by managers, not engineers. Every number must have: - A unit ('days', 'items', '%') - A plain English label ('Items currently waiting in Code Review' not 'flow_load by status') - A 'so what' implication where severity is high (use conditional template logic) Do NOT use these terms without explanation: Flow Efficiency, Flow Time, WIP, Lead Time, Cycle Time, CFD, DORA. If used, always append the explanation in parentheses. ## Key code references - data/report/domain_metrics.py: calculate_flow_metrics() — follow same pattern for calculate_flow_analysis_metrics() - data/report/generator_metrics.py: where metrics dict is assembled — add flow_analysis key - report_assets/report_section_flow.html: existing flow section style to follow for HTML structure - report_assets/report_macros.html: available macros (color coding, badges) - report_assets/report_section_bugs.html: example of narrative + table pattern to follow - report_assets/report_section_recommendations.html: table structure with severity badges ## Architecture rules - data/report/domain_metrics.py: pure data loading and transformation, no HTML - report_assets/: Jinja2 templates only, no Python logic beyond simple conditionals - Full type annotations on all Python functions - Max function size 50 lines - No emoji anywhere - Commit must reference: burndown-chart-xmd7, burndown-chart-h322 (this bead's ID)
title: Flow Analysis: report section for manager narrative

notes:
REPORT GAP ANALYSIS - Executive Stakeholder Needs

## Current Report (4 sections)
✅ Dashboard: Health score, progress, velocity, forecast
✅ Burndown: Chart, scope changes, bug analysis
✅ Budget: Consumption, burn rate, runway, cost efficiency
✅ Flow/DORA: Process metrics with tier benchmarking

## Critical Gaps for Executive Decisions

### Budget Decisions
MISSING: Budget vs forecast alignment, risk thresholds, what-if scenarios
NEED: 'Money runs out BEFORE/AFTER work completes' indicator

### Deadline Decisions  
MISSING: Confidence intervals, schedule risk level, required actions
NEED: 'Forecast DATE ±X days (75% confidence) - Medium Risk'

### Resource Decisions
MISSING: Velocity trend interpretation, bottleneck ID, capacity analysis
NEED: 'Team velocity ↗️ improving 15% (CV: 20% stable)'

### Quality Decisions
MISSING: Quality impact on schedule/budget, severity breakdown
NEED: 'Bugs consuming 30% capacity, adding 4 weeks to forecast'

## Top 11 Recommendations

HIGH PRIORITY (Phase 1 - Week 1):
1. Executive Summary (health, forecast, budget, top 3 risks/wins)
2. Risk Assessment Card (schedule/budget/quality/scope risk levels)
3. Budget-Forecast Alignment (runway vs completion comparison)
4. Health Score Breakdown (6 dimensions: what's weak/strong)

MEDIUM PRIORITY (Phase 2 - Week 2):
5. Actionable Insights (severity badges, expandable recommendations)
6. Burndown interpretation (on track? slope analysis?)
7. Bug impact analysis (capacity %, schedule impact, severity)
8. Scope risk assessment (deadline threat? context-aware)

LOW PRIORITY (Phase 3 - Week 3):
9. Simplify Flow tables (3 tables → 1 summary + chart)
10. Add comparative context (trends: better/worse over time)
11. Remove redundancies (velocity shown twice, low-value metrics)

## Key Finding
Report excels at technical metrics but lacks executive storytelling. 
Stakeholders get raw data without interpretation or decision-support.

TARGET: Self-service report where PM/CTO/CEO can make informed 
budget/deadline/resource decisions WITHOUT asking PM for translation.

SUCCESS METRIC: Report generates <3 follow-up questions (vs current ~10)
EFFORT: 3-4 weeks (phased implementation)

notes:
DECISION: KEEP update_reconnect.js

EVALUATION SUMMARY:
✅ Implementation is clean and reliable
✅ All integration tests pass (4/4)
✅ Complexity is manageable (3 files, ~75 LOC total)
✅ UX benefit is significant (single tab vs 2 tabs after update)
✅ Edge cases handled gracefully (missing DB, concurrent access)

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:
- updater/updater.py: 41 lines (flag write + error handling)
- app.py: 29 lines (flag read + cleanup)
- tests/integration/test_update_workflow.py: 200 lines (4 comprehensive tests)

KEY STRENGTHS:
1. Uses existing database infrastructure (no new dependencies)
2. Atomic operations with SQLite (no race conditions)
3. Graceful degradation (falls back to normal launch if flag fails)
4. One-time flag (auto-cleanup prevents state pollution)
5. System tray 'Open in Browser' still works (flag only affects auto-launch)

EDGE CASES COVERED:
- Missing database file → app launches normally
- Concurrent flag access → SQLite locking handles it
- Flag cleanup failure → next launch clears it anyway
- Updater crash before flag write → app launches normally

RECOMMENDATION: Feature complete and production-ready. Close xmc and deploy.
title: Decision: Keep or scrap update_reconnect.js based on implementation complexity

description:
Based on research findings, design a solution for updating both BurndownChart.exe and BurndownChartUpdater.exe.

Requirements:
- Both executables must be updated atomically
- Handle Windows file locking (cannot replace running executable)
- Prevent corruption if update interrupted
- Backward compatible with existing update flow
- Clear rollback strategy on failure

Consider approaches:
1. Two-phase update (app updates updater first, then updater updates app)
2. Temporary updater copy that updates both files
3. App-managed updater replacement before launching updater
4. Delayed file operations (MoveFileEx with MOVEFILE_DELAY_UNTIL_REBOOT)

Deliverable: Design document in specs/self-updating-updater/design.md with:
- Architecture diagrams
- Update flow sequence
- Error handling strategy
- File operation order
- Rollback mechanism
title: Design self-updating updater architecture

close_reason: Closed
description: Persist update download state to app_state table. Store: pending_update_version, pending_update_path, pending_update_url. Restore on startup if file exists. Clear after successful update. Implement in update_manager.py and app.py.
notes:
Implementation details:

Problem: Download state lost on app restart. User downloads ~100MB ZIP → app crashes → Windows cleans %TEMP% → state lost. Current: app.VERSION_CHECK_RESULT is in-memory only.

Database keys (app_state table):
- pending_update_version (str): e.g., '1.2.3'
- pending_update_path (str): e.g., 'C:\Users\...\AppData\Local\Temp\burndown_updates\BurndownChart-1.2.3.zip'
- pending_update_url (str): Download URL for re-download fallback
- pending_update_size (int): File size in bytes (for progress bar)
- pending_update_checked_at (str): ISO timestamp (for staleness check, >7 days → re-check)

Code changes:
1. data/update_manager.py: Save to database on download complete via backend.set_app_state()
2. app.py: Restore on startup, verify file exists, populate app.VERSION_CHECK_RESULT
3. Clear after successful update (atexit handler or updater flag)

Fallback: If file missing → set status AVAILABLE (re-download required, no crashes)
title: Implement download state persistence to database

close_reason: Closed
description: Implement graceful degradation when pending_update_path file deleted from %TEMP%. Check file existence on startup, fall back to AVAILABLE state if missing. No crashes or error dialogs, just require re-download.
notes:
Implementation details:

Problem: Windows Disk Cleanup or user manually deletes files from %TEMP%, breaking update state.

Solution: Graceful degradation with file existence checks.

Code location: app.py startup, in restore_pending_update() function.

Logic:
- Restore pending_update_path from database
- Check if Path(path_str).exists()
- If exists: Set VERSION_CHECK_RESULT status='READY' (downloaded, ready to install)
- If missing: Set VERSION_CHECK_RESULT status='AVAILABLE' (need to re-download)
- Clear stale database keys: backend.set_app_state('pending_update_path', None)
- Log warning but no error dialogs

Behavior matches industry standard (Chrome, Firefox): Missing download → fall back to 'download again' state.

Testing: Delete ZIP from %TEMP% → restart app → verify UI shows download button (not install button)
title: Add fallback logic for missing temp files

close_reason: Closed
description:
Implement system tray icon for console-free executable to provide user control and visibility.

REQUIREMENTS:

1. System Tray Icon:
   - Appears in Windows system tray when app runs
   - Uses app icon (assets/icon.ico)
   - Shows on hover: 'Burndown Chart - Running on http://127.0.0.1:8050'

2. Context Menu Actions:
   - 'Open in Browser' - Opens/focuses http://127.0.0.1:8050 in default browser
   - 'Quit' - Gracefully shuts down app (sys.exit(0))

3. Implementation Details:
   - Library: pystray (https://pypi.org/project/pystray/)
   - Add to requirements.in: pystray>=0.19.5
   - Tray icon runs in separate daemon thread
   - Must not block Dash/Waitress server
   - Icon file: assets/icon.ico (already exists for executable)

4. Files to Modify:
   - requirements.in - add pystray dependency
   - app.py - initialize tray icon when running as frozen executable
   - build/app.spec - ensure icon.ico is bundled (already is)

5. Code Structure:
   `python
   if getattr(sys, 'frozen', False):  # Only for executable
       import pystray
       from PIL import Image
       
       def on_open(icon, item):
           webbrowser.open('http://127.0.0.1:8050')
       
       def on_quit(icon, item):
           icon.stop()
           sys.exit(0)
       
       icon = pystray.Icon('burndown-chart',
           Image.open('assets/icon.ico'),
           'Burndown Chart',
           menu=pystray.Menu(
               pystray.MenuItem('Open in Browser', on_open),
               pystray.MenuItem('Quit', on_quit)
           ))
       
       # Run in separate thread
       threading.Thread(target=icon.run, daemon=True).start()
   `

6. Testing Checklist:
   - [ ] Tray icon appears when app starts
   - [ ] Hover shows app name and URL
   - [ ] 'Open in Browser' launches/focuses browser tab
   - [ ] 'Quit' cleanly shuts down app
   - [ ] Icon disappears when app exits
   - [ ] No tray icon when running 'python app.py' (source mode)
   - [ ] Works with update flow (old icon stops, new icon appears)

BENEFITS:
- User can always reopen browser if tab closed
- Clean way to quit without Task Manager
- Visibility that app is running
- Professional UX like mailhog, Docker Desktop, etc.

DEPENDENCIES:
- pystray: System tray icon library
- PIL/Pillow: Image handling (already installed via other deps)

BLOCKED BY:
- burndown-chart-rk4 (update UX improvements must be tested first)
title: Add system tray icon for background app control

description:
Add dual-metric support to DORA MTTR card showing both full lifecycle (customer to production) and team cycle time (backlog to resolution), following Deployment Frequency pattern.

## Requirements

### Type Classification (DORA Metrics)
- Add 'Bug Types' field in Type tab alongside existing 'Incident Types'
- Maps to development project bug issue type

### Field Mappings (4 total)
1. 'Incident Detected At' -> Problem type, created (DevOps project) [EXISTING]
2. 'Incident Deployed At' (renamed from 'Incident Resolved At') -> Problem type, fixVersions (DevOps)
3. 'Incident Created At' (NEW) -> Bug type, created (Development project)
4. 'Incident Resolved At' (NEW) -> Bug type, resolutiondate (Development project)

### Chart Display
- Follow Deployment Frequency card pattern exactly
- Two lines: 'MTTR Lead Time' (primary) and 'MTTR Cycle Time' (secondary)
- Show both main chart and scatter chart for each metric
- Treat Problem and Bug data as independent points

### Card Statistics
- Primary: MTTR Lead Time average (Problem: created -> fixVersions)
- Secondary: MTTR Cycle Time average (Bug: created -> resolutiondate)
- If only one configured: show one line + placeholder for unmapped metric
- Layout MUST NOT change based on configuration

### Waiting Time Metrics
- Add 'Waiting for Development' (if Problem->created and Bug->created available)
- Add 'Waiting for Deployment' (if Bug->resolutiondate and Problem->fixVersions available)
- Display in card when data available

### Compatibility
- Support all existing field mappings (fixVersions, resolutiondate, etc.)
- Maintain backward compatibility with current configurations
notes:
## Code Analysis

**MTTR Calculation** (data/dora_metrics.py:L1190-1320):
- Single metric: incident_detected_at  incident_resolved_at
- Supports 2 end states: resolutiondate (team fix) OR fixVersions (deployment)
- Uses _extract_datetime_from_field_mapping() for field extraction
- Imports fixversion_release_map for deployment date matching

**Field Mappings** (configuration/dora_config.py:L128):
- REQUIRED_DORA_FIELDS['mean_time_to_recovery'] = ['incident_detected_at', 'incident_resolved_at']
- Single field pair  needs 4 fields for dual metrics

**Type Classification** (ui/issue_type_config_form.py:L158-198):
- 'Incident Types' dropdown  bug_types field
- Maps to incident issues for MTTR/CFR
- Need parallel 'Bug Types' field for development project bugs

**Chart Rendering** (ui/metric_cards.py:L424-470):
- Deployment Frequency: create_dual_line_trend() shows 2 lines (deployments vs releases)
- Pattern: weekly_values (primary) + weekly_release_values (secondary)
- MTTR: Uses standard single-line chart  needs dual-line upgrade

**Visualization** (visualization/dora_charts.py:L441-520):
- create_deployment_frequency_trend(): Dual traces (deployments + releases)
- Primary: operational tasks, Secondary: unique fixVersions
- MTTR trend: Single trace  replicate dual pattern

**Field Extraction** (callbacks/field_mapping.py:L1713-1714):
- incident_detected_at, incident_resolved_at labels
- Need: incident_created_at, new incident_resolved_at (rename old to incident_deployed_at)

**Implementation Path**:
1. Add 2 new field mappings (incident_created_at, incident_resolved_at for Bugs)
2. Rename incident_resolved_at  incident_deployed_at
3. Add bug_types field in Type tab
4. Extend dora_metrics.py MTTR calc for dual data extraction
5. Pass both datasets to visualization layer
6. Update create_mttr_chart() following deployment_frequency pattern
7. Add waiting time calculations (Problem.created - Bug.created, Bug.resolutiondate - Problem.fixVersions)
title: Extend MTTR card with Lead Time and Cycle Time metrics

description:
## Purpose
Wire the Flow Analysis tab into the app: register the tab, write the callback that loads issues and calls the data layer, connect the sort control, and fix the three pre-existing structural bugs discovered during design analysis. Depends on burndown-chart-xmd7 (data) and burndown-chart-ldjf (UI).

## What to build

### 1. New tab registration
Add 'Flow Analysis' tab to the main tab bar. Follow the same pattern as the 'Current Work' (active_work_timeline) tab. Tab label icon suggestion: fa-route or fa-stream.

Key files to check for tab registration pattern:
- app.py: main tab definitions
- callbacks/__init__.py: callback module registration
- ui/ tab layout assembly (check how active_work_timeline tab is registered)

### 2. New file: callbacks/flow_analysis.py
Single callback that renders the full Flow Analysis tab content.

Inputs/triggers (follow active_work_timeline.py pattern):
- Input: tab selection (triggers on tab becoming active)
- Input: data-points-slider value (re-render on change)
- Input: open-items sort dropdown value
- Input: completed-items sort dropdown value
- State: active profile/query from app state

Callback logic (delegate to data/, never put logic in callbacks/):
1. Load issues: backend.get_issues(profile_id, query_id)
2. Load settings: load_app_settings() to get field mappings
3. Extract: flow_end_statuses, flow_wip_statuses, active_statuses from settings
4. Filter development issues: filter_development_issues() (same as active_work_timeline)
5. Call: get_flow_analysis_data(issues, flow_end_statuses, flow_wip_statuses, active_statuses, data_points_count)
6. Call UI: create_flow_analysis_summary_bar(result['summary'])
7. Call UI: create_open_items_section(result['open_items'], open_sort_key)
8. Call UI: create_completed_items_section(result['completed_items'], completed_sort_key)
9. Return assembled layout

Empty/error states: return appropriate empty state card (follow active_work_timeline empty state pattern).

### 3. Fix pre-existing wire bug: flow_metrics not passed to insights engine
During design analysis, three structural bugs were found. Fix all three in this bead.

Bug A — flow_metrics never passed to create_insights_section():
File: ui/dashboard/__init__.py (around line 416)
Current call:
    create_insights_section(
        statistics_df,
        settings,
        budget_data,
        pert_data=pert_data,
        deadline=settings.get('deadline') if settings else None,
    )
Fix: extract flow/bug/dora metrics from settings['extended_metrics'] and pass them:
    extended = settings.get('extended_metrics', {})
    flow_m = extended.get('flow') if extended else None
    bug_m = extended.get('bug_analysis') if extended else None
    dora_m = extended.get('dora') if extended else None
    create_insights_section(
        statistics_df,
        settings,
        budget_data,
        pert_data=pert_data,
        deadline=settings.get('deadline') if settings else None,
        flow_metrics=flow_m,
        bug_metrics=bug_m,
        dora_metrics=dora_m,
    )

Bug B — key name mismatch between extended_metrics loader and correlation_signals consumer:
File: callbacks/visualization_helpers/extended_metrics.py, function _try_load_flow()
Current output keys: 'wip', 'flow_time'
Expected by data/recommendations/correlation_signals.py _add_h4_wip_lead_time(): 'avg_wip', 'median_flow_time'
Fix: rename keys in the dict assigned to extended_metrics['flow']:
    extended_metrics['flow'] = {
        'has_data': True,
        'velocity': avg_velocity,
        'avg_wip': wip,          # was: 'wip'
        'median_flow_time': median_flow_time,  # was: 'flow_time'
        'efficiency': avg_efficiency,
        'work_distribution': { ... },
    }
    Keep the old keys as aliases for backward compatibility if the report templates use them:
        'wip': wip,          # backward compat alias
        'flow_time': median_flow_time,  # backward compat alias

Bug C — extended_metrics['flow'] has 'has_data' key but H4 guard checks flow_metrics.get('has_data') while the dict from extended_metrics loader sets it correctly. Verify this is working after Bug A and B are fixed by tracing through the signal. Add a debug log line in _add_h4_wip_lead_time() that logs the wip and lead_time values it received (at DEBUG level).

### 4. Register callback module
Add callbacks/flow_analysis.py to callbacks/__init__.py import list (same pattern as other callback modules).

## Key code references
- callbacks/active_work_timeline.py: full callback pattern to follow for tab render
- callbacks/__init__.py: module registration pattern
- ui/dashboard/__init__.py L416: Bug A location
- callbacks/visualization_helpers/extended_metrics.py _try_load_flow(): Bug B location
- data/recommendations/correlation_signals.py _add_h4_wip_lead_time(): Bug C verification
- data/project_filter.py: filter_development_issues() to reuse

## Architecture rules
- callbacks/ layer: event routing only, delegate all logic to data/ and ui/
- No business logic, no calculations, no file I/O directly in callbacks/flow_analysis.py
- Full type annotations on all functions
- Max function size 50 lines
- No emoji in logs or comments
- Commit must reference all 3 bead IDs: burndown-chart-xmd7, burndown-chart-ldjf, burndown-chart-XXXX (this bead's ID)
notes: CORRECTION: This bead's own ID is burndown-chart-h322. The commit message in the description says 'burndown-chart-XXXX (this bead ID)' — replace XXXX with h322. Report bead ID is burndown-chart-af47. Full bead sequence: xmd7 (data) -> ldjf (UI) -> h322 (callback+wire) -> af47 (report).
title: Flow Analysis: callback, tab registration, and existing signal wire-up

description:
Panels (settings, parameters, data) on mobile viewports appear to be cut off when content exceeds screen height, with no vertical scroll capability.

**Problem:**
- Opening panels like Settings, Parameters, or Data on mobile devices
- When panel content is longer than mobile screen height, content is cut off
- No vertical scroll bar appears for panel content
- User cannot access content below the fold

**Investigation Needed:**
- Test in actual mobile devices vs browser mobile emulation
- Verify if issue is browser-specific or genuine mobile problem
- Check CSS overflow properties on panel containers
- Review modal/panel container height and scroll settings
- Check if mobile_navigation.js or viewport_detection.js interferes

**Technical Areas to Check:**
- Modal/offcanvas scroll behavior in Dash Bootstrap Components
- CSS in custom.css affecting panel containers
- Viewport height calculations on mobile
- Touch scroll event handling

**Expected Behavior:**
- Panels should scroll vertically when content exceeds viewport height
- Scroll bars should be visible or touch-scrolling should work
- All panel content should be accessible on mobile devices
title: Fix mobile UI panel scrolling issues

close_reason: Completed comprehensive report design overhaul with velocity-card system, responsive grids, and symmetric layouts
description: Complete redesign of HTML report template with consistent velocity-card design system, responsive grids, and symmetric layouts. All report sections now follow unified design language with left-accent cards, proper spacing, and color-coded elements.
notes:
DESIGN SYSTEM CHANGES:
- Unified velocity-card design: All metric cards use left-border accent (4px), consistent padding, box shadows
- Responsive grids: Flow metrics 2x2, DORA metrics 2x2, adaptive auto-fit for Budget/Scope cards
- Color palette: Brand blue (#0d6efd), orange, green, red, purple for different metric types

HEALTH OVERVIEW SYMMETRY:
- Removed ITEMS/POINTS text labels, kept only badges (matching HEALTH layout)
- Badge colors: Blue for ITEMS, orange for POINTS (match ring colors)
- All three columns now identical structure: gauge/ring → badge → progress details
- Changed health status badge text to white for all states (CAUTION/GOOD/AT RISK)

HEALTH SCORE BREAKDOWN:
- Moved percentages inside progress bars with smart positioning (min 20% position for small bars)
- Dark pill badges with white text showing exact percentage
- Fixed Jinja2 type errors (3 iterations): split inline conditionals into separate if/else blocks
- Removed needle indicator attempt (z-index issues)

SECTION REORGANIZATION:
- Removed Risk Assessment section (redundant with Health Score Breakdown)
- Moved velocity cards before Health Score Breakdown
- Standardized ALL section headers: H4 with icons (chart-line, calendar-week, chart-area, chart-bar)

BUG ANALYSIS:
- Reorganized to single 3-card row: Open Bugs (red), Resolution Rate (green), Expected Resolution (blue)
- Consolidated Expected Resolution: '~13 weeks (6.50 per week)' format
- Removed separate Target/Rate lines

BUDGET SECTION:
- Moved Cost Efficiency cards (Cost Per Item, Cost Per Point) above Budget-Forecast Alignment
- Format: '€260.47 (4-week velocity avg)' in velocity-card-unit span
- Increased Consumed progress bar: 25px → 35px height, larger text (1.1rem)
- Single-line alerts: 'Budget Shortfall: X weeks (Forecast: Y | Runway: Z)'
- Center-aligned all budget alerts
- Removed 'Budget-Forecast Alignment' section title
- Fixed leaking escaped \\n after section title
- Reduced margin on budget revisions message (mb-0)

FLOW METRICS:
- Changed from adaptive grid to fixed 2x2: grid-template-columns: repeat(2, 1fr)
- Centered 'Work Distribution' title
- 4 cards: Flow Velocity (blue), Flow Time (orange), Flow Efficiency (green), Flow Load/WIP (purple)

DORA METRICS:
- Changed to 2x2 grid layout (matching Flow metrics)
- 4 cards: Deployment Frequency (blue), Lead Time (green), Change Failure Rate (warning), MTTR (critical)

SCOPE SECTION:

close_reason: Implemented
description:
## Goal
Split three files from the data/report/ and visualization/ layers that exceed 900 lines.

## Files to split

### 1. data/report/chart_burndown.py (1001L)
Run: rg -n '^def |^class ' data/report/chart_burndown.py
Expected split based on chart type:
- data/report/chart_burndown_daily.py   -- daily burndown/burnup chart functions
- data/report/chart_burndown_weekly.py  -- weekly breakdown chart functions (including generate_weekly_breakdown_chart which is ~366L itself)
- data/report/chart_burndown_pert.py    -- PERT/forecast chart functions
Keep data/report/chart_burndown.py as a re-export shim.

### 2. data/report/generator.py (988L)
Run: rg -n '^def |^class ' data/report/generator.py
Expected split:
- data/report/generator_metrics.py       -- metrics aggregation
- data/report/generator_recommendations.py -- calculate_recommendations (596L function -- this is the Big one)
- data/report/generator_assembly.py      -- HTML report assembly and rendering
Keep data/report/generator.py as a re-export shim.

### 3. visualization/forecast_chart.py (940L)
Run: rg -n '^def |^class ' visualization/forecast_chart.py
Expected split:
- visualization/forecast_chart_traces.py  -- trace/series builders
- visualization/forecast_chart_layout.py  -- layout, axes, annotation builders
- visualization/forecast_chart_render.py  -- top-level create_forecast_plot entry point
Keep visualization/forecast_chart.py as a re-export shim.

## Process for each file
1. Read the full file. Use rg to list all def/class.
2. Cluster by responsibility. Keep related private helpers with their public function.
3. Create new canonical modules with docstrings and type hints.
4. Replace original file with shim (see data/processing.py for exact pattern).
5. Run: python validate.py --fast
6. Run: python -m pytest tests/ -x -q --tb=short
7. Fix all import errors. Re-run until clean.
8. Commit: refactor(arch): split oversized report and visualization files (bd-XXXX)

## Rules
- Behavior-preserving only. No logic changes.
- noqa: F401 on all shim re-exports.
- Lazy imports inside functions (e.g. generate_weekly_forecast) must be preserved exactly as-is.
- Max 500 lines per new file. If a single function exceeds 300L, put it alone in its own module.
title: Split oversized data/report and visualization files (>900L)

close_reason: Fixed by tracking rendered percentage and adjusting remaining segment width
description:
## Bug Description
Some sprint progress bars appear shorter than others, not reaching the full width. All bars should end at the same position since they share the same sprint end date.

## Root Cause
Two issues in _create_multi_segment_bar():

1. When segments < 0.3% were skipped, we recalculated remaining_percentage as (100 - total_rendered_percentage), which varied per issue
2. The remaining gray segment should be the SAME for all issues (based on sprint dates), not recalculated per issue

This caused different bars to show different remaining percentages (6%, 6.5%, 6.4%, etc) when they should all be identical.

## Fix Applied
- Track total_rendered_percentage for issue segments
- Calculate gap_before_issue = time_progress_percentage - total_rendered_percentage
- Add gap as light gray segment at beginning (if > 0.1%)
- Use ORIGINAL remaining_percentage (from sprint calculation) for end segment
- This ensures all bars end at exactly the same position

## Files Changed
- visualization/sprint_charts.py: Fixed remaining percentage calculation

## Verification
- All 19 tests pass in test_sprint_charts.py
- All bars now align at right edge (same remaining percentage)
title: Sprint progress bars render shorter than 100% width

close_reason: Implemented in commit 5438a92c: proactive field value prefetch on modal open
description:
## Problem

In 'Configure Jira Mappings':
- Types tab > Effort Categories dropdown: empty on fresh install
- Environment tab > Production Identifiers dropdown: empty on fresh install

Both dropdowns show no values until the user runs 'Update Data' at least once. This creates a chicken-and-egg problem during first-time setup: the user cannot configure these mappings before fetching data.

## Root Cause (Code Research Findings)

### How values are populated (current flow)

Values for both dropdowns come from  etched-field-values-store (Dcc.Store), which is:
1. Cleared on every modal open (callbacks/field_mapping/modal_core.py line 81)
2. Only populated via  etch_field_values_on_blur callback (callbacks/field_value_fetch.py line 143)
3. That callback only fires when the user **blurs** the namespace field inputs for ffort_category or ffected_environment in the Fields tab

### Value resolution priority (callbacks/field_mapping/tab_rendering.py)

**Effort categories (Types tab):**
1.  etched-field-values-store['effort_category'] - only exists if user blurred field this session
2. metadata.field_options[effort_category_field] - populated during metadata fetch, but only if field was mapped BEFORE metadata was fetched

**Production identifiers (Environment tab):**
1. metadata.field_options[affected_environment_field] - same limitation
2.  etched-field-values-store['affected_environment'] - same limitation
3. state_data.field_values.target_environment - requires a prior 'Update Data' run (populated by auto-configure from issue analysis)
4. metadata.auto_detected.production_identifiers - auto-detection from pattern matching, gives empty list on fresh install

### fetch_field_options() already tries Jira API

data/jira/metadata_fetcher.py::fetch_field_options() already implements a 4-strategy chain:
1. Jira Cloud context+options API: GET /api/{v}/field/{fieldId}/context then GET /api/{v}/field/{fieldId}/context/{contextId}/option
2. Field schema allowedValues: GET /api/{v}/field/{fieldId}
3. Legacy endpoint: GET /api/{v}/customFieldOption/{fieldId}
4. Extract from local jira_cache.json (requires prior Update Data)
5. JQL query sampling up to 1000 issues (requires projects configured and Jira connectivity)

**For select/option-type fields** (which both effort_category and affected_environment often are), strategy 1 can return values WITHOUT any prior data fetch.

## Why it doesn't work on first open

The problem is not that the Jira API cannot return values -- it often can. The problem is that  etch_field_options() is never called when the modal opens with fields already configured. It is only called reactively (on blur of the field input).

## Jira API Research (Cloud v3 + Server)

### Cloud: Select field options (no issue data needed)
- GET /rest/api/3/field/{fieldId}/context - get field contexts (requires Administer Jira or read:field.option:jira scope)
- GET /rest/api/3/field/{fieldId}/context/{contextId}/option - paginated list of all options for that context
- This works for any select/multi-select custom field and returns options defined in field configuration, not from actual issue data
- Limitation: requires Administer Jira global permission on Cloud; on Server it may require project admin

### Server/Data Center
- Similar context API available at GET /rest/api/2/field/{fieldId}/context
- Older Server instances may only have the legacy GET /rest/api/2/customFieldOption/{optionId} (single option lookup, not enumeration)

### Fields backed by free-form text or labels
- No enumeration API exists - must query issues to discover actual values in use
- JQL sampling (_fetch_field_values_from_issues) is the only option

## Proposed Fix Approaches

### Option A (Preferred): Proactive fetch on modal open / tab switch
When the modal opens OR when user switches to Types/Environment tab:
- If ffort_category field is already mapped, immediately call  etch_field_options() and populate the store
- If ffected_environment field is already mapped, do the same
- This reuses the existing multi-strategy fetch chain (works for select fields without issue data; falls back to JQL for text fields)
- Implementation: add a new callback or extend modal_core open callback to trigger fetches for already-mapped fields

### Option B: Fetch on metadata load
During the metadata fetch (which already happens when Jira config is saved/updated), also call  etch_field_options() for the configured effort_category and affected_environment fields and store results in metadata.field_options. Already partially done, but only runs if the fields were mapped before metadata was fetched.

### Option C: Trigger fetch when Fields tab values are set (not just on blur)
Extend  etch_field_values_on_blur to also handle 
_submit or trigger on tab switch from Fields tab to Types/Environment tab.

## Affected Files
- callbacks/field_value_fetch.py - add proactive fetch on modal open
- callbacks/field_mapping/modal_core.py - trigger field value pre-fetch on open
- callbacks/field_mapping/tab_rendering.py - wire pre-fetched values into tab rendering
- callbacks/jira_metadata.py - optionally fetch options during metadata load (line 106+)
- data/jira/metadata_fetcher.py - fetch_field_options() (already correct, no changes needed)
title: Effort categories and production identifiers empty on first configuration

close_reason: Fixed silent update failures. Implemented unique extraction dirs, updater logging, auto-cleanup, and consistent naming. Tested and committed (ade0e52).
description:
**Bug**: Update from v2.6.2 to v2.6.3 fails silently. Download succeeds, .bak file created, but update stops at copy phase. Root cause: stale temp directories from previous failed updates block new extractions.

**Root Cause Analysis**:
- Static folder name 'burndown_update_temp' causes conflicts
- Existing folders not cleaned before extraction
- Updater output went to /dev/null (no error visibility)
- Generic naming pattern risks collision with other apps

**Fixes Implemented**:
1. Unique extraction directories: burndown_chart_update_{uuid} (prevents conflicts)
2. Updater logging: Output to %TEMP%\burndown_chart_updater.log (diagnostic visibility)
3. Automatic cleanup: Remove orphaned folders >1hr old on app startup
4. Consistent naming: All temp resources use 'burndown_chart_*' namespace
5. Fail-fast: Abort if cleanup fails (defensive programming)

**Files Changed**:

description:
## Purpose
Build the Dash/HTML UI components that render individual item status paths as horizontal swimlane rows. Depends on burndown-chart-xmd7 (data layer). No callbacks yet.

## Context from design session
The page shows each item as a horizontal sequence of colored status blocks, width proportional to time spent, left-to-right chronologically. Completed items end at their done status. Open items end at 'now' with the current status visually distinguished (pulsing border or bold label). Technical users (scrum master, PM, agile coach) — tooltips are sufficient explanation, no over-explanation on page.

## What to build

### New file: ui/flow_analysis.py
All component builder functions for the Flow Analysis tab. No callbacks, no data loading — pure rendering from pre-computed dicts.

#### Function: create_flow_analysis_tab_layout() -> html.Div
Top-level layout shell for the tab. Contains:
1. Page header: 'Flow Analysis' with subtitle 'Item journey through workflow statuses'
2. Controls row: data points slider reference (read from shared store, not owned here), sort dropdown (see below)
3. Summary bar (see create_flow_analysis_summary_bar())
4. Section 1: Open items (see create_open_items_section())
5. Section 2: Completed items (see create_completed_items_section())

#### Function: create_flow_analysis_summary_bar(summary: dict) -> html.Div
Compact metric bar at top showing aggregate signals from summary dict (produced by data/flow_analysis.py get_flow_analysis_data()):
- Total open items
- Items waiting 5+ days in current status (count, highlighted red if >0)
- Bottleneck status name + avg days items wait there
- Avg total journey days (completed items)

Single row of 4 stat cards, consistent with other summary rows in the app (see ui/budget_timeline_compact.py for reference style).

#### Function: create_sort_control(section: str) -> dbc.Select
Dropdown for sort order. section is 'open' or 'completed'.
For open items, options:
- 'current_wait' (default, label: 'Longest in current status')
- 'total_journey' (label: 'Longest total journey')
- 'transitions' (label: 'Most status changes (rework)')
For completed items, options:
- 'longest_stage' (default, label: 'Longest single status')
- 'total_journey' (label: 'Longest total journey')
- 'transitions' (label: 'Most status changes (rework)')

#### Function: create_item_path_row(item: dict, show_points: bool = False) -> dbc.Card
Single item rendered as a horizontal status path. The item dict is one entry from open_items or completed_items (produced by get_issue_status_path()).

Row structure (top to bottom):
1. Header line: [key badge] [summary text] [parent/epic badge if present] [current status badge] [days in current status badge for open items | total journey badge for completed]
2. Status path: horizontal flex row of status blocks
   - Each block width proportional to duration_days (use percentage of total journey)
   - Minimum block width: 40px (so very short stages are still visible)
   - Color coding:
     * Active status (is_active_status=True): green (#198754 or Bootstrap success)
     * Wait/queue status (is_wait_status=True, not active): amber (#fd7e14 or Bootstrap warning)
     * Done/completed terminal status: grey (#6c757d or Bootstrap secondary)
     * Current status for open items: add bold left border (3px solid) to distinguish
   - Each block shows status name (truncated if <60px) and duration in days on hover tooltip
   - For open items, the current status block shows 'X days' label directly (not just tooltip)

Signals on header (matching active_work_manager thresholds already used in app):
- is_blocked signal (5+ days in current status, open items): red warning badge 'Blocked'
- is_aging signal (3-5 days, open items): amber badge 'Aging'
- high transition count (>5 transitions): blue badge 'Rework'

#### Function: create_open_items_section(open_items: list[dict], sort_key: str) -> html.Div
Renders section 1. Sorts open_items by sort_key before rendering:
- 'current_wait': sort by current_status_duration_days DESC
- 'total_journey': sort by total_duration_days DESC
- 'transitions': sort by transition_count DESC
Shows count in section header: 'Open Items (N)'.
If empty: show empty state card 'No open items in selected period'.

#### Function: create_completed_items_section(completed_items: list[dict], sort_key: str) -> html.Div
Renders section 2. Sorts completed_items by sort_key:
- 'longest_stage': sort by longest_status_days DESC
- 'total_journey': sort by total_duration_days DESC
- 'transitions': sort by transition_count DESC
Shows count in section header: 'Completed Items (N)'.
Shows data points window in subtitle: 'Completed within last N weeks'.
If empty: show empty state card 'No completed items in selected period'.

## Key code references
- ui/active_work_components.py: badge patterns, status badge styling — follow same conventions
- ui/bug_analysis.py: create_quality_insights_panel() — card structure pattern
- ui/budget_timeline_compact.py: compact stat row reference
- data/active_work_manager.py: is_blocked threshold (5 days), is_aging threshold (3 days) — DO NOT re-implement, reference same constants
- assets/active_work_timeline.css: existing status color classes — reuse where possible
- assets/active_work_search.css: badge and layout patterns

## New CSS
Add to assets/flow_analysis.css (new file):
- .flow-status-block: base block style (flex, min-width, padding, border-radius)
- .flow-status-path: flex container (display: flex, align-items: stretch, gap: 4px, overflow-x: auto)
- .flow-status-active: green block
- .flow-status-wait: amber block  
- .flow-status-done: grey block
- .flow-status-current: bold left border override
- .flow-item-row: card margin/padding for item rows
- .flow-summary-stat: stat card in summary bar

## Architecture rules
- ui/ layer only — no data loading, no callbacks, no imports from callbacks/
- All functions must have full type annotations
- Max function size 50 lines — split into helpers freely
- No emoji in code, logs, or comments
- No hardcoded status names or colors outside the color-coding logic above
- Follow BEM-like CSS naming: .flow-* prefix for all new classes
title: Flow Analysis: UI components for item path view

description:
## Goal
The current coverage gate is a single global threshold (--cov-fail-under).
Add per-layer coverage measurement with explicit targets for data/, ui/, callbacks/, and visualization/.

## Step 1: Measure per-layer coverage baseline
Run the following to get per-package coverage:
  .venv\Scripts\python.exe -m pytest tests/ --cov=data --cov=ui --cov=callbacks --cov=visualization --cov-report=term-missing -q 2>&1

Note the TOTAL % for each package from the output table.

## Step 2: Update validate.py check_coverage() function
Read the existing check_coverage() function in validate.py.
Add a second coverage run (or extend the existing one) that:
1. Measures per-package coverage with --cov=data --cov=ui --cov=callbacks --cov=visualization
2. Generates a JSON report: --cov-report=json:coverage.json
3. Parses coverage.json to extract per-package totals
4. Logs each package coverage like: [validate] coverage: data=72% ui=58% callbacks=34% visualization=61%
5. Fails if data/ drops below 65%, or if any layer drops by more than 5pp from its baseline

Targets based on expected baselines:
- data/:         fail if < 65%  (data layer is most unit-tested)
- ui/:           fail if < 45%  (many UI components are hard to test without browser)
- callbacks/:    fail if < 30%  (Dash callbacks are hard to unit-test)
- visualization/:fail if < 50%

## Step 3: Add coverage.json to .gitignore
Run: rg 'coverage' .gitignore
If coverage.json is not already excluded, add it.

## Step 4: Validate the full gate still passes
Run: .venv\Scripts\python.exe validate.py --fast
Confirm all checks pass.

## Step 5: Commit
git commit -m 'ci(coverage): add per-layer coverage targets to validation gate (bd-XXXX)'

## Rules
- Do NOT break the existing --cov-fail-under global threshold -- add per-layer checks ON TOP of it.
- Keep validate.py under 600 lines.
- The per-layer check should print clearly even when passing, so coverage trends are visible in CI logs.
- coverage.json should never be committed to the repository.
title: Add per-layer coverage targets to CI gate

description:
## Goal
The pre-push gate enforces --cov-fail-under=43, but the actual coverage is higher.
Update the threshold to prevent future regression and add per-layer coverage visibility.

## Steps

### 1. Measure actual coverage
Run: .venv\Scripts\python.exe -m pytest tests/ --cov=data --cov=ui --cov=callbacks --cov=visualization --cov-report=term-missing --cov-report=json -q 2>&1 | Select-String 'TOTAL|data|ui|callbacks|visualization'

Note the TOTAL line percentage and per-package values.

### 2. Update validate.py coverage threshold
Open validate.py and find the check_coverage() function.
Change: --cov-fail-under=43
To: --cov-fail-under=<ACTUAL_TOTAL - 2> (round down to nearest integer, leave 2% slack)

Also add explicit source flags if not already present:
--cov=data --cov=ui --cov=callbacks --cov=visualization

### 3. Update the docstring/comment in validate.py
Update the comment that says '~60%' to reflect the actual number found in step 1.

### 4. Verify the gate passes with new threshold
Run: .venv\Scripts\python.exe validate.py --fast
Confirm: [validate] pytest (coverage): OK

### 5. Commit
git add validate.py
git commit -m 'ci(coverage): raise coverage threshold to measured baseline (bd-XXXX)'

### 6. Push and confirm pre-push gate passes
git push

## Rules
- Do NOT raise the threshold higher than (actual - 2). Leave slack so legitimate code additions do not immediately break CI.
- Do NOT reduce the threshold below 50.
- If actual coverage is already above 70%, target (actual - 3) for more breathing room.
- After updating, run the full gate once to confirm: python validate.py
title: Raise coverage threshold to current baseline

close_reason: Fixed toast alignment by adding app-toast class to JavaScript-created update success toast
description:
Layout bug: The 'Successfully Updated' message displayed after an update completes is positioned incorrectly and goes out of bounds.

ROOT CAUSE:
The JavaScript-created toast in assets/update_reconnect.js (line ~137) is missing the 'app-toast' CSS class that all Python-generated toasts have. This class provides sizing constraints that work with the container's positioning.

TECHNICAL DETAILS:

Container Positioning (ui/layout.py lines 141-145):
- position: fixed
- top: 5px
- right: -132px (shifts container 132px to the left)
- zIndex: 9999
- width: 520px

description:
Refactor data/persistence/sqlite_backend.py from 1861 lines to comply with 500-line architectural guideline.

## Current Analysis
**File**: data/persistence/sqlite_backend.py
**Size**: 1861 lines (3.7x over 500-line limit)
**Functions**: 3 module functions + 1 massive SQLiteBackend class

## Initial Assessment
- **Helper functions**: _extract_nested_field, retry_on_db_lock (< 100 lines)
- **SQLiteBackend class**: Implements PersistenceBackend interface (~1700 lines)
  - CRUD operations for all entities (profiles, queries, settings, statistics, snapshots, etc.)
  - Database initialization and migration
  - Transaction management
  - Likely 40-60 methods

## Proposed Split Strategy
1. **data/persistence/sqlite/** subdirectory:
   - base.py - SQLiteBackend base class + __init__, connection management (< 300 lines)
   - profiles.py - Profile CRUD operations (< 300 lines)
   - queries.py - Query CRUD operations (< 300 lines)
   - settings.py - Settings CRUD operations (< 250 lines)
   - statistics.py - Statistics CRUD operations (< 350 lines)
   - snapshots.py - Snapshot CRUD operations (< 250 lines)
   - field_mappings.py - Field mapping CRUD operations (< 250 lines)
   - migrations.py - Schema initialization and migrations (< 300 lines)
   - helpers.py - _extract_nested_field, retry_on_db_lock (< 150 lines)
   
2. Use Python mixins pattern:
   `python
   class SQLiteBackend(
       ProfileMixin,
       QueryMixin,
       SettingsMixin,
       StatisticsMixin,
       SnapshotsMixin,
       FieldMappingMixin,
       MigrationMixin,
       BaseSQLiteBackend
   ):
       pass
   `

## Tasks
1. Analyze SQLiteBackend methods and group by entity
2. Create mixin classes for each entity type
3. Extract migration logic to separate mixin
4. Implement composition pattern
5. Split into 9 focused files (each < 350 lines)
6. Update imports
7. Run tests to verify database operations work

## Success Criteria
- All files < 500 lines (target: 200-350)
- Clear entity separation using mixins
- Maintainable class composition
- All tests pass
- Zero errors from get_errors
title: Refactor data/persistence/sqlite_backend.py (1861 lines → <500)

description:
On first run of the packaged executable, extract the project's LICENSE.txt file to the application directory alongside the exe. This ensures users have easy access to license terms without needing to open the executable bundle.

Implementation:
- Check if LICENSE.txt exists in current directory on startup
- If not found and running as frozen executable, extract from bundled resources
- Place LICENSE.txt in same directory as BurndownChart.exe
- Log extraction for troubleshooting

Note: Third-party licenses are bundled inside the executable and accessible via PyInstaller's resource system. Legal requirement is typically to make them ACCESSIBLE (which they are), not necessarily extract them. They remain in the bundle and can be accessed programmatically if needed (e.g., Help -> View Licenses menu option in future).

Related: Portable executable - first run acts as installation/setup.
title: Extract LICENSE.txt on first run

close_reason: Feature abandoned - AI assistant not feasible
description: Knowledge base loads 657K chars (~164K tokens) into 4K token context window, causing immediate crash. Need to: 1) Load only relevant snippets based on question, 2) Truncate to max ~2000 tokens for context, 3) Leave room for conversation history
notes:
CRASH ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED:
- Deduplication working (prevents Enter+Click double-fire)
- BUT: User pressing Send button MULTIPLE times during 24s response generation
- Multiple concurrent llama.create_chat_completion() calls -> crash (not thread-safe)

SOLUTIONS TO TRY:
1. Disable Send button during response generation (UI approach)
2. Add processing lock to prevent concurrent generation calls (backend approach)
3. Queue messages instead of concurrent execution
4. Show 'Generating...' indicator to discourage multiple clicks

LOGS SHOW:
- 23:25:25 - First send (button click 1)
- 23:25:50 - Second send (button click 2) - 25s later, DURING first generation
- 23:26:04 - Third send (button click 3) - 14s later, STILL processing
- Result: 3 concurrent llama calls -> crash

PREFER: Option 1 (disable button) - simplest, best UX
title: Fix knowledge base context overflow causing AI assistant crash

close_reason: Completed and released in v2.12.0 - changelog data included in profile export/import
description:
After introduction of Sprint Tracker and Active Work Timeline features, changelog entries are not included in profile export/import. This creates data portability issues.

**Impact:**
- Sprint Tracker cannot build sprint snapshots without sprint field changelog
- Sprint Tracker missing time-in-status calculations (status changelog)
- Active Work Timeline missing health indicators (WIP detection, last status change)
- Imported profiles show 'No sprint changelog data found' error

**Current Export Coverage:**
✓ Profile data, query metadata, issues, statistics, metrics, budget
✗ Changelog entries (jira_changelog_entries table)

**Sprint Tracker Dependencies:**
- backend.get_changelog_entries(profile_id, query_id, field_name=sprint_field)
- backend.get_changelog_entries(profile_id, query_id, field_name='status')
- Used in: callbacks/sprint_tracker.py (lines 140-160, 294)
- Data: sprint snapshots, add/remove tracking, progress bars

**Active Work Dependencies:**
- backend.get_changelog_entries(profile_id, query_id, issue_key=key, field_name='status')
- Used in: data/active_work_manager.py (lines 342-396)
- Data: last status change, WIP detection, health indicators

**Required Changes:**

1. Export (data/import_export.py, export_profile_with_mode):
   - Add backend.get_changelog_entries() calls in FULL_DATA mode
   - Export sprint field changelog and status changelog
   - Add to query_data[query_id]['changelog_entries']
   - Filter by tracked fields (sprint, status) to reduce size

2. Import (callbacks/import_export.py, perform_import):
   - Read changelog_entries from query_data
   - Call backend.save_changelog_batch() to restore
   - Handle field name mapping (customfield IDs differ per instance)

3. Considerations:
   - Changelog can be large (implement field filtering)
   - Sprint field ID mapping (customfield_10005 vs customfield_10020)
   - Make changelog optional like budget data (include-changelog checkbox)
   - Update export manifest to track changelog inclusion

**Files to modify:**
- data/import_export.py (export_profile_with_mode function)
- callbacks/import_export.py (perform_import function)
- ui/import_export.py (add include-changelog checkbox option)

**Database Table:**
jira_changelog_entries: profile_id, query_id, issue_key, field_name, old_value, new_value, change_date, author, expires_at

**Testing:**
- Export profile with Sprint Tracker data
- Import to new instance
- Verify sprint snapshots rebuild correctly
- Verify Active Work health indicators work
title: Add changelog export/import for Sprint Tracker and Active Work

close_reason: Implemented retry logic, grace period, and elapsed time logging for AV interference handling

Added retry logic with exponential backoff (20 attempts over ~60s), grace period after process exit, and elapsed time logging. Paradoxically works on non-admin accounts but fails with admin rights due to elevated process security scanning.
title: Fix updater file locking on admin accounts (AV interference)

close_reason: Fixed legend overlap by increasing bottom margin and adjusting legend position
description:
**Problem**: Bug Analysis tab charts have legends overlapping with rotated x-axis labels and 'Week' title, especially on mobile view.

**Root Cause**: 
- Mobile layout bottom margin (50px) too small for: rotated 45° labels (~60px) + axis title (~20px) + legend (~50px)
- Legend y-positions (-0.15, -0.2, -0.3) positioned too high, causing overlap

**Solution**:
1. Increased mobile bottom margin: 50px → 130px
2. Adjusted legend y-position: -0.15/-0.2/-0.3 → -0.35 (consistent across all bug charts)
3. Slightly increased mobile height: 400px → 450px to accommodate larger margin
4. Applied to: get_mobile_chart_layout(), create_bug_trend_chart(), create_bug_investment_chart()

**Testing**: All 45 bug tests + 5 visual layout tests passing
title: Fix Bug Analysis legend overlap with x-axis labels

description:
## Goal
Split three large UI utility files that exceed 900 lines.

## Files to split

### 1. ui/tooltip_utils.py (957L)
Run: rg -n '^def |^class ' ui/tooltip_utils.py
Expected split by tooltip domain:
- ui/tooltip_utils_core.py    -- core hoverlabel/template functions (create_hoverlabel_config, format_hover_template)
- ui/tooltip_utils_charts.py  -- chart-specific tooltip builders
- ui/tooltip_utils_cards.py   -- card/metric tooltip builders
Keep ui/tooltip_utils.py as a re-export shim.

NOTE: create_hoverlabel_config and format_hover_template are imported by visualization/ modules.
After splitting, verify those visualization callers still work -- they already import from ui.tooltip_utils,
so no caller changes needed (shim handles it).

### 2. ui/loading_utils.py (913L)
Run: rg -n '^def |^class ' ui/loading_utils.py
Expected split:
- ui/loading_utils_core.py      -- base loading overlay and skeleton functions
- ui/loading_utils_patterns.py  -- pattern-specific loading states (chart, table, card)
Keep ui/loading_utils.py as a re-export shim.

### 3. visualization/bug_charts.py (893L)
Run: rg -n '^def |^class ' visualization/bug_charts.py
Expected split:
- visualization/bug_charts_trend.py    -- bug trend over time charts
- visualization/bug_charts_forecast.py -- bug forecast charts (generate_bug_weekly_forecast)
- visualization/bug_charts_distribution.py -- bug distribution/breakdown charts
Keep visualization/bug_charts.py as a re-export shim.

## Process for each file
1. Run rg -n '^def |^class ' on each file to list all public symbols.
2. Read through file to group by domain/responsibility.
3. Create canonical modules with docstrings, type hints, proper imports.
4. Replace original with shim (see data/processing.py for exact shim pattern).
5. Run: python validate.py --fast
6. Run: python -m pytest tests/ -x -q --tb=short
7. Fix all import errors. Re-run until clean.
8. Commit: refactor(arch): split oversized UI utility files (bd-XXXX)

## Rules
- Behavior-preserving only. No logic changes.
- noqa: F401 on all shim re-exports.
- Max 500 lines per new file.
- After splitting ui/tooltip_utils.py, verify visualization/ callers still import successfully.
title: Split oversized UI utility files (>900L)

description:
Refactor data/persistence/adapters.py from 1888 lines to comply with 500-line architectural guideline.

## Current Analysis
**File**: data/persistence/adapters.py
**Size**: 1888 lines (3.8x over 500-line limit)
**Functions**: 20+ persistence adapter functions

## Initial Assessment
Contains multiple persistence domains:
1. **Helpers**: _get_file_lock, DateTimeEncoder class, convert_timestamps_to_strings, should_sync_jira
2. **App Settings**: save_app_settings, load_app_settings
3. **Project Data**: save_project_data, load_project_data, save_unified_project_data, load_unified_project_data
4. **Settings**: save_settings, load_settings
5. **Statistics**: save_statistics, save_statistics_from_csv_import, load_statistics
6. **Sample Data**: generate_realistic_sample_data, read_and_clean_data
7. **Scope**: get_project_statistics, get_project_scope, update_project_scope
8. **Migration**: _migrate_legacy_project_data

## Proposed Split Strategy
1. **data/persistence/adapters/** subdirectory:
   - core.py - Helper functions, DateTimeEncoder, file locks (< 200 lines)
   - app_settings.py - save_app_settings, load_app_settings (< 350 lines)
   - project_data.py - save_project_data, load_project_data, unified functions (< 450 lines)
   - settings.py - save_settings, load_settings (< 200 lines)
   - statistics.py - All statistics save/load functions (< 400 lines)
   - sample_data.py - generate_realistic_sample_data, read_and_clean_data (< 250 lines)
   - scope.py - get_project_statistics, get_project_scope, update_project_scope (< 150 lines)
   - migrations.py - _migrate_legacy_project_data (< 150 lines)
   
2. **data/persistence/adapters/__init__.py** - Export all public functions

## Tasks
1. Identify shared dependencies between adapter functions
2. Group by data domain (app settings, project data, statistics, etc.)
3. Split into 8 focused files (each < 500 lines)
4. Update 30+ import statements across codebase
5. Maintain backward compatibility
6. Run tests to verify persistence works

## Success Criteria
- All files < 500 lines (target: 150-450)
- Clear domain separation
- All imports updated
- All tests pass
- Zero errors from get_errors
title: Refactor data/persistence/adapters.py (1888 lines → <500)

description:
## Goal
Replace generic dict return types with TypedDict for the five most-used data shapes.
This improves IDE autocompletion, pyright accuracy, and makes data contracts explicit.

## TypedDicts to define

Create a new file: data/types.py
This file defines all shared TypedDicts. Other modules import from here.

### 1. AppSettings
Used by: data/persistence/, callbacks/settings/, callbacks/visualization_helpers/
Fields to discover: run rg 'app_settings\[' data/ callbacks/ -l to find all key accesses, then define fields.
Minimum expected fields: profile_id, query_id, jira_url, jira_token, jira_project, jql, data_window_weeks, sprint_length_weeks, working_days_per_week, points_field, sprint_field

### 2. JiraConfig
Used by: data/jira/, callbacks/settings/data_update.py
Fields to discover: run rg 'jira_config\[' data/jira/ -l
Minimum expected fields: api_endpoint, token, jql, custom_fields, projects

### 3. MetricsResult
Used by: data/report/dashboard_metrics.py, callbacks/visualization_helpers/
Discover fields: run rg 'metrics\[|result\[' data/report/ -l

### 4. SprintSnapshot
Used by: data/sprint_snapshot_calculator.py, callbacks/sprint_tracker.py
Discover fields by reading: data/sprint_snapshot_calculator.py

### 5. QueryProfile
Used by: data/jira/query_profiles.py, callbacks/settings/query_profiles.py
Discover fields by reading the profile schema in data/schema.py or data/persistence/

## Implementation steps
1. Create data/types.py with all five TypedDicts. Use total=False for optional fields.
2. Update return type annotations on the 3-5 most critical functions per TypedDict:
   - Functions that construct and return these shapes (not all callers)
   - Focus on data/ layer functions only for now
3. Run: .venv\Scripts\python.exe validate.py --fast
4. Fix any pyright errors (use Optional[] for nullable fields).
5. Run: python -m pytest tests/unit/data/ -x -q --tb=short
6. Commit: refactor(types): add TypedDict definitions for core data shapes (bd-XXXX)

## Rules
- data/types.py MUST have no imports from data/ subdirectories (no circular risk).
- Only import from: typing, typing_extensions, datetime, pandas
- Do NOT change any function logic -- only update type annotations.
- Use total=False for TypedDicts with optional fields (most will have some).
- Keep data/types.py under 200 lines.
title: Add TypedDict definitions for top 5 dict shapes

description:
## Goal
Replace generic 'except Exception' catches in the data/ layer with a typed exception hierarchy.
This makes error propagation explicit and enables callers to distinguish error categories.

## Step 1: Create data/exceptions.py
Define the following hierarchy (keep file under 80 lines):

    class BurndownBaseError(Exception):
        'Base class for all burndown-chart domain exceptions.'

    class JiraError(BurndownBaseError):
        'JIRA API connectivity, auth, or query errors.'

    class JiraAuthError(JiraError):
        'JIRA authentication failed.'

    class JiraQueryError(JiraError):
        'JIRA JQL query failed or returned unexpected data.'

    class PersistenceError(BurndownBaseError):
        'Database or file persistence failures.'

    class MetricsError(BurndownBaseError):
        'Calculation or data processing errors in metrics.'

    class ConfigurationError(BurndownBaseError):
        'Invalid or missing configuration.'

    class CacheError(BurndownBaseError):
        'Cache read/write errors.'

## Step 2: Replace generic catches in data/jira/
Find: rg 'except Exception' data/jira/ -l
For each file, read the context around each 'except Exception' and replace:
- JIRA auth failures -> JiraAuthError
- JIRA API/HTTP failures -> JiraError
- Query/JQL failures -> JiraQueryError
Keep 'except Exception' ONLY at the outermost function boundary as a last-resort fallback,
and make sure it logs the full exception with logger.exception() not just logger.error().

## Step 3: Replace generic catches in data/persistence/
Find: rg 'except Exception' data/persistence/ -l
Replace with PersistenceError where appropriate.
SQLite OperationalError and IntegrityError should be caught specifically before the generic catch.

## Step 4: Replace in data/cache_manager.py
data/cache_manager.py has 7+ generic catches. Replace with CacheError where appropriate.

## Step 5: Validate
Run: .venv\Scripts\python.exe validate.py --fast
Run: python -m pytest tests/unit/data/ tests/integration/ -x -q --tb=short
Fix any import errors (import from data.exceptions in each file that raises/catches).

## Step 6: Commit
git commit -m 'refactor(errors): introduce domain exception hierarchy in data layer (bd-XXXX)'

## Rules
- data/exceptions.py must have zero imports from other data/ modules (no circular risk).
- Do NOT remove existing error logging -- keep all logger.error/exception calls.
- Callbacks/ layer should NOT import from data/exceptions for now -- that is a follow-up task.
- If replacing a catch changes behavior (e.g. it was silently swallowed), log it clearly first.
title: Introduce domain exception hierarchy in data layer

close_reason: Moved shared tooltip and JIRA link helpers to utils, updated visualization imports to remove ui-layer dependencies, added UI compatibility shims, and validated with targeted tests plus full pre-push gate.
description:
## Goal
visualization/ modules import from ui/ (9 instances), creating a downward dependency violation.
The architectural rule is: visualization/ is a pure chart-generation layer with no ui/ dependency.
Fix this by moving the shared utilities to utils/ where both layers can import them.

## Step 1: Identify everything being imported from ui by visualization
Run: rg 'from ui\.' -g '*.py' visualization/ | ForEach-Object {  }
Expected findings:
- ui.tooltip_utils: create_hoverlabel_config, format_hover_template (imported by 9+ visualization/ files)
- ui.jira_link_helper: create_jira_issue_link (imported by 2+ visualization/ and ui/ files)

## Step 2: Move shared symbols to utils/
Create utils/chart_tooltip_utils.py:
- Copy create_hoverlabel_config, format_hover_template from ui/tooltip_utils.py
- Add module docstring explaining this is the shared chart-layer version
- Keep the originals in ui/tooltip_utils.py -- they will become re-exports pointing here

Create utils/jira_link_utils.py (or add to utils/url_utils.py if it exists):
- Copy create_jira_issue_link from ui/jira_link_helper.py
- Keep original in ui/jira_link_helper.py as a re-export

## Step 3: Update visualization/ callers
For each visualization/ file importing from ui.tooltip_utils:
  from ui.tooltip_utils import create_hoverlabel_config, format_hover_template
Change to:
  from utils.chart_tooltip_utils import create_hoverlabel_config, format_hover_template

For visualization/ files importing from ui.jira_link_helper:
  from ui.jira_link_helper import create_jira_issue_link
Change to:
  from utils.jira_link_utils import create_jira_issue_link

## Step 4: Update ui/ shims to re-export from utils/
In ui/tooltip_utils.py, add re-exports for the moved symbols:
  from utils.chart_tooltip_utils import create_hoverlabel_config, format_hover_template  # noqa: F401

In ui/jira_link_helper.py:
  from utils.jira_link_utils import create_jira_issue_link  # noqa: F401

This preserves backward compat for all ui/ callers.

## Step 5: Verify no remaining violations
Run: rg 'from ui\.' visualization/ | ForEach-Object {  }
Expected: empty output.

## Step 6: Validate and commit
Run: .venv\Scripts\python.exe validate.py --fast
Run: python -m pytest tests/ -x -q --tb=short
Fix any import errors.
git commit -m 'refactor(arch): move shared tooltip/link utils out of ui layer (bd-XXXX)'

## Rules
- Do NOT change function signatures or logic -- copy only, no modifications.
- All ui/ callers must continue to work via the re-export shim.
- utils/ modules must have no imports from ui/ or visualization/.
title: Move tooltip_utils and jira_link_helper out of ui layer

close_reason: Completed toast styling optimization: reduced padding from 24px to 16px, added 14px font-size to header, increased minWidth from 280px to 300px to prevent header wrapping. Changes are backward compatible and use design system variables for consistency.
description:
**Problem**: Toast message headers often wrap to 2 lines, making toasts unnecessarily large due to empty space and oversized text.

**Goals**:
- Reduce font size in toast messages (both header and body)
- Reduce unused empty space/padding inside toasts
- Increase toast width by ~20px to prevent header wrapping
- Keep position fixed at top-right corner of screen

**Reason**: Current toasts take up too much vertical space when headers wrap, reducing screen real estate and looking cluttered.

**Implementation**: Adjust CSS in prevent_credentials_popup.css or custom.css for .toast elements.
notes:
## Technical Analysis

### Current Implementation

#### 1. Toast Component Structure
- File: ui/toast_notifications.py (lines 24-132)
- Function: create_toast() - Creates dbc.Toast components
- Default minWidth: 280px (hardcoded inline style, line 131)
- Content structure: Icon + message wrapped in html.Div

#### 2. Toast Container Location
- File: ui/layout.py (lines 141-149)
- Container ID: 'app-notifications'
- Position: Fixed at top: 5px, right: 5px
- Width: 350px (container width)
- Z-index: 9999 (highest layer)

#### 3. Current CSS Styling
- File: assets/custom.css (lines 5867-5888)
- Toast header: padding: var(--space-3) = 1.5rem = 24px
- Toast body: padding: var(--space-3) = 1.5rem = 24px
- Toast header font: font-weight: var(--font-semibold) = 600
- Toast body font: font-size: var(--text-sm) = 0.875rem = 14px
- Border radius: var(--radius-lg) = 0.5rem = 8px
- Shadow: var(--shadow-lg)

#### 4. Design System Variables (custom.css lines 1-200)
- --space-3: 1.5rem (24px) - Current padding
- --space-2: 1rem (16px) - Smaller padding option
- --space-1: 0.5rem (8px) - Minimal padding option
- --text-sm: 0.875rem (14px) - Body text (already used)
- --text-xs: 0.75rem (12px) - Smaller text option
- --font-semibold: 600 - Current header weight
- --font-medium: 500 - Lighter weight option

### Proposed Changes (Non-Breaking)

#### 1. Reduce Padding
**Target**: .toast-header and .toast-body
- Change from: padding: var(--space-3) [24px]
- Change to: padding: var(--space-2) [16px] OR padding: var(--space-1) var(--space-2) [8px 16px]
- Impact: Reduces vertical space by ~16-32px per toast

#### 2. Reduce Font Sizes
**Target**: .toast-header
- Add: font-size: var(--text-sm) [14px] (currently inherits larger size)
- Keep font-weight: var(--font-semibold) for emphasis
**Target**: .toast-body (already 14px)
- Optional: font-size: var(--text-xs) [12px] for more compact body

#### 3. Increase Toast Width
**Target**: ui/toast_notifications.py line 131
- Change from: minWidth: '280px'
- Change to: minWidth: '300px' (~20px increase as requested)
**Alternative**: Adjust container width in ui/layout.py line 148
- Change from: width: '350px'
- Change to: width: '370px'

#### 4. Header Wrapping Prevention
- Increasing minWidth by 20px should reduce wrapping for most headers
- Alternative: Add CSS max-width and text-overflow: ellipsis for extremely long headers
- Keep position: fixed, top: 5px, right: 5px (as requested)

### Implementation Files
1. assets/custom.css (lines 5874-5888) - Padding and font size changes
2. ui/toast_notifications.py (line 131) - minWidth inline style
3. Optional: ui/layout.py (line 148) - Container width adjustment

### Risk Assessment
- **Low Risk**: CSS padding/font changes (easily reversible)
- **Very Low Risk**: Width adjustments (only affects toast container)
- **No Breaking Changes**: All existing toast calls continue to work
- **Backward Compatible**: Design system variables ensure consistency
title: Optimize toast message styling to reduce size and prevent header wrapping
